author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/test/0/test_90
body:
Why do intelligent, highly qualified women freely choose to work in the City of London? It is a question that might preoccupy a visiting anthropologist from a more rational culture, observing the native females as they submit, with apparent willingness, to an environment that is, by all accounts, brutish, sexist, and designed to repel anyone not in possession of a rhinoceros-hide. Whatever the motivation—money, presumably, or the thrill of being shouted at by men in braces, or the promise of bonuses that dwarf the GDP of small nations—the environment is, as we now know, ghastly. Though not, it must be said, quite as ghastly as that endured by women in certain police forces, fast-food chains, men's magazines, mini-brothels, or the Church of England Synod. The City, at least, has the decency to pay its victims six-figure salaries before humiliating them, and to offer the occasional lapdance as a form of corporate entertainment.

Public knowledge of this ghastliness comes, almost exclusively, from a parade of discrimination and unfair-dismissal claims, without which the City might still pose as a model equal-opportunities employer, its diversity policies framed and hung in reception, its annual reports glossy with photographs of smiling female graduates. The lineage of City complainants can be traced back, with some precision, to Nicola Horlick in 1997, a press-savvy martyr who reframed a grubby staff-poaching dispute into a moral crusade, dragging entranced reporters to Frankfurt and back. She was quickly rehabilitated, her martyrdom rewarded with a new job and a book deal, and her example set a precedent: suffering, in the City, could be a career move. Successors have demanded ever-larger sums to compensate for bullying, spitting, name-calling ("hot totty"), and the occasional lapdancing incident. One might almost admire the entrepreneurial spirit, were it not for the collateral damage to the English language.

Consider Claire Bright, who is seeking £11m from HBOS, where she earned £600,000 a year. She allegedly suffered under a man she has called "a mini Hitler". Given the size of a City office versus Nazi Germany, one can only observe, with dark comedy, that only a "mini-Holocaust" was achievable—perhaps involving the confiscation of paperclips and the occasional aggressive email. The scale of grievance inflation is breathtaking. Not to be outdone, six Dresdner Kleinwort Wasserstein bankers are collectively demanding £800m for offences that include being called "the Pamela Anderson of trading" (a compliment, surely, in some quarters) and witnessing a CEO date his assistant. One struggles to imagine the trauma of witnessing a superior's romantic life—the stolen glances, the furtive lunches—but at these prices, one must suppose it is exquisite, a wound that only an eight-figure sum can salve. One might also mention the case of a former Merrill Lynch banker who claimed £1.2m after being called a "bimbo"—a term that, in the City, apparently carries a premium.

Now, contrast these figures with the tariffs set by the Criminal Injuries Compensation Board. Victims of the July 7 bombings who lost limbs received roughly £50,000 per leg. A leg, one might think, is worth rather more than the distress of being compared to a Baywatch star, but the market, it seems, disagrees. By the City's calculus, a limb is worth approximately 0.0006% of a Dresdner claim. One might ask whether the bombers, had they targeted a bank, would have faced higher tariffs—a sort of progressive compensation system, with bonuses for financial-sector victims. Should newspapers even advertise such gargantuan claims? They bear little relation to real-world settlements, and may inflict further pain on the "still-traumatised" victims—and their lawyers, who must weep into their silk handkerchiefs at the injustice of it all. The newspapers that report these claims with straight faces are the same ones that editorialise about a "compensation culture" when a tripper on a pavement receives a few thousand. Consistency, like a limb, is undervalued. The City, it seems, has perfected the art of monetising indignity, while the truly injured must make do with the spare change.

**Can schools be protected from forces of darkness?**

From the City's compensation culture to the education sector's amateur hour, the common thread is institutional hypocrisy. A Guardian advertisement placed by Oasis UK, the charity of the Rev Steve Chalke, recently sought an "accomplished fundraiser" to realise a vision of "outstanding schools and community hubs" rooted in Christian values. The jargon is instructive: the successful candidate will be a "HVD" (high value dealer), a term one might associate with drug trafficking rather than education. The ad, which appeared in that beacon of secular values, asked for someone to "maximise income from high net worth individuals"—or, in the demotic, to fleece the rich for Jesus. Neither the fundraiser nor Chalke, one notes, appears to need any school-running experience. Faith, presumably, will supply the missing expertise, along with a certain muscular intolerance.

Rev Chalke, a man who once compared homosexuality to drug addiction, is now entrusted with shaping young minds. His vision of "outstanding schools" presumably includes outstanding intolerance, a curriculum in which biology is optional and the sinfulness of certain lifestyles is a core subject. The government, in its infinite wisdom, has decided that professional teachers are overrated. Instead, we have a parade of enthusiasts: the homophobic religious fanatics, the self-promoting tradesmen (Sir Reg Vardy, who made his fortune selling cars and his reputation denying evolution), the would-be barons (Lord Harris of Peckham, carpet king turned academy sponsor), the parents who think that because they once helped with homework they can run a school, and the classroom assistants promoted beyond their competence. Consider Peter Hyman, Tony Blair's former speechwriter, who decamped to Islington Green "to do something different"—a phrase that might also describe a mid-life crisis involving a motorbike and a leather jacket, but in his case meant teaching, despite having no qualifications. He was, after all, a man who had written speeches about "education, education, education" without ever having taught a class. Or Reg Vardy, the creationist school sponsor, whose grasp of biology must be as firm as his grasp of pedagogy; his schools, one assumes, teach that the Flintstones is a documentary. One wonders whether children's minds will be safe in such hands, or whether they will emerge believing that the earth is 6,000 years old and that the City is a feminist utopia. Will they be able to distinguish between a fact and a faith-based assertion? Or will they, like their sponsors, believe that women deserve lower pay because Eve was made from a rib? One might as well ask a City banker to run an ethics course.

**This week**

I have been reading Naomi Wolf's The Treehouse, a memoir of her father, a poet, and his attempts to build a treehouse. It is, by all accounts, a meditation on creativity and family. But it made me yearn for the practical wisdom of Little House in the Big Woods, where Pa Ingalls could build a cabin, skin a deer, and play the fiddle, all without a creative writing degree or a TED talk. His life lessons seem superior, and his treehouse, if he ever built one, would presumably have been structurally sound. I have also, shamefully, been enjoying George Galloway's self-destruction on Celebrity Big Brother. There is something compelling about watching a man who once lectured the world on morality, and who still finds time to praise Saddam Hussein, reduced to pretending to be a cat while dressed in a leotard. It is, one might say, the City of London of reality television: a place where dignity is surrendered for a fee, and where the compensation, in terms of public humiliation, is incalculable. Between the City's compensation culture and the government's faith in amateur educators, one detects a common theme: the belief that expertise is overrated, and that a large cheque or a strong conviction can substitute for competence.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_2
body:
It is a truth universally acknowledged, among the dwindling band of loyalists who still gather in the virtual snug of the political sketch-writers’ WhatsApp group, that a magazine profile is the most exquisite form of political obituary. The longer the lead time, the more poignant the corpse. This week, the *New Statesman* has produced a cover story of such spectacularly mistimed devotion that it should, by rights, be framed and hung in the Strangers’ Bar as a *memento mori* for any politician who has ever been tempted to believe his own press. The subject is Sir Keir Starmer, and the headline, “The Man Who Would Be King,” sits above a photograph of the Labour leader gazing into the middle distance with the sort of pensive, statesmanlike squint that suggests he is either contemplating the moral arc of the universe or trying to remember if he left the hob on.

Inside, the profile is a masterpiece of the genre. We learn that Starmer has undergone a “remarkable transformation,” that he has “found his voice,” and that his “forensic” style—that word again, the political equivalent of a doctor’s reassuring murmur before the fatal diagnosis—is at last cutting through the Westminster din. A shadow cabinet colleague is quoted, anonymously, as saying that Starmer is “the most focused leader we’ve had since Blair,” which is a bit like being told you’re the most buoyant passenger since the *Titanic*’s captain. The piece is larded with the usual stage directions: Starmer, we are informed, has swapped his “stiff” suits for something “more relaxed,” has learned to “banter” with journalists, and now laughs “easily” at himself. The overall effect is of a man who has been painstakingly assembled by a team of engineers from the Institute for Acceptable Leadership, and who is now ready to be wheeled out for public inspection.

The only problem, and it is a rather large one, is that the *New Statesman* went to press approximately fifteen minutes before the roof fell in. Between the final sign-off and the magazine’s arrival on the nation’s doormats, the Labour Party contrived to lose two by-elections in seats so safe that the local wildlife had reportedly begun to evolve webbed feet. The first, in a constituency whose name I have already forgotten—it was something like Midsomer Central—saw a Labour majority of 20,000 dissolve into a Tory gain, while the second, in a northern town where the party’s vote was once weighed rather than counted, produced a swing that would have been considered indecent in a pendulum. The morning after, the front pages were a gallery of Starmer’s misery: “Keir We Go,” said the *Sun*, with the sort of punning cruelty that is, in its way, a form of respect. The *Daily Mail* went with “Starmer’s Nightmare,” and even the *Guardian*, which has never knowingly understated a crisis, simply printed a photograph of the Labour leader looking like a man who has just been told his winning lottery ticket was purchased in a parallel universe.

The sketch-writers, of course, had a field day. Quentin Letts, the *Times*’s parliamentary Eeyore, described Starmer as “a human Resources department in a suit,” while the *Telegraph*’s Madeline Grant observed that the Labour leader “now resembles a man who has been told to smile by a focus group and has misunderstood the assignment.” The “forensic” style, once hailed as the antidote to Boris Johnson’s bluster, was suddenly being recast as a kind of emotional anaemia. Where once Starmer was “relentless,” he was now “plodding.” Where once he was “incisive,” he was now “pedantic.” The very qualities that had been polished to a high sheen in the *New Statesman* profile had, in the space of a weekend, curdled into liabilities. It was as if a washing machine, purchased with a glowing *Which?* recommendation, had begun to shred the laundry the moment the warranty expired.

The washing machine metaphor is, I think, the only one that will do. The modern political party treats its leaders as consumer durables: they are acquired with great fanfare, praised for their energy efficiency and sleek design, and then, at the first sign of a funny noise, dumped at the municipal tip without a backward glance. The Labour Party, in particular, has developed a genius for this cycle. It is only a few years since Jeremy Corbyn was being hailed as the saviour of the left, a man whose “authenticity” and “principles” would galvanise a new generation of voters. Before him, Ed Miliband was the “intellectual” who would out-think the Tories, right up until the moment he was photographed eating a bacon sandwich and the nation collectively decided he was unfit for office. Each leader is, in his moment, the subject of the same breathless profiles, the same anonymous briefings, the same assurances that this time, finally, the party has found its man. And each leader, in turn, is discarded with the same brutal efficiency, his name added to the landfill of political history, where it will slowly decompose alongside those of Kinnock, Foot, and Brown.

The environmental implications of this are, frankly, alarming. If we continue to dispose of party leaders at the current rate, the British countryside will soon be dotted with vast, toxic mounds of discarded politicians, their “forensic” skills and “relentless” determination leaching into the water table. The next leader, whoever he or she may be, will have an even shorter shelf life than Starmer, because the public’s patience is now calibrated in months rather than years. The *New Statesman* will, no doubt, produce another cover story, with another headline about another man who would be king, and the sketch-writers will sharpen their pencils, and the whole grisly cycle will begin again. The only consolation is that, by the time the planet is finally overwhelmed by the accumulated waste of political ambition, there will be no one left to read the profiles.

**The Cruelty of the House System**

It is a curious fact, and one that has been insufficiently remarked upon in the recent obituaries of the late, great Paul Foot, that the British left owes its existence almost entirely to the private education system. Foot, who died this week, was a product of Shrewsbury School, an institution whose principal contribution to the nation’s political life has been to produce a steady stream of radicals, dissidents, and investigative journalists, all of whom seem to have been forged in the white heat of the house system. The logic is inescapable: without the petty cruelties of fagging, the arbitrary tyrannies of prefects, and the cold, institutionalised misery of the dormitory, the British left would have been deprived of its most powerful motivating force. The young Foot, forced to endure the indignities of a public school education, emerged with a lifelong hatred of injustice and a determination to expose the workings of power. It is, in its way, a heartwarming story.

But what are we to make of the contemporary trend towards softer, more “pastoral” private schools, where the house system has been replaced by something called “vertical tutoring” and the prefects are now known as “peer mentors”? If the theory holds, the next generation of left-wing firebrands will be so emotionally well-adjusted that they will have no reason to storm the barricades. The recent example of the singer and songwriter, let us call him, for legal reasons, “Jarvis Cocker,” is instructive. Cocker, a product of the moderately strict City School in Sheffield, has made a career out of a kind of sardonic, anti-establishment dandyism that would have been impossible without the formative experience of being told off for wearing the wrong socks. If the government’s current enthusiasm for competitive school sport—a policy promoted with characteristic zeal by the Education Secretary—succeeds in its aim of building “character,” it may well succeed in producing a generation of revolutionaries. The irony, one feels, would be lost on the minister, who will be too busy polishing his own profile to notice the tumbrils rolling down Whitehall.

**The Friendship Lottery**

A new report from the Social Integration Commission has found that British adults have, on average, fewer than three close friends from a different ethnic background. The report, which was accompanied by the usual graphs and the usual calls for “action,” has prompted me to conduct a brief audit of my own social circle. The results are, I am afraid, a disgrace. Not only do I lack the requisite number of ethnically diverse friends, I am also grievously deficient in Welsh friends, estate agent friends, baronet friends, and friends who have ever expressed a sincere interest in the sport of curling. My social circle is, in fact, a monument to homogeneity, a kind of human monoculture that would make a biodiversity expert weep.

The question, of course, is what is to be done. The report’s authors suggest that employers should encourage “friendship leave,” a proposal that conjures up the image of a harassed office worker filling out a form requesting a half-day off to “consolidate a cross-cultural bond.” But this is surely not enough. If the government is serious about social integration, it should introduce a national friendship lottery, in which every citizen is randomly assigned twenty new friends from a range of demographic categories. The system would be administered by a quango, naturally, and there would be a helpline for those who found themselves assigned to a baronet. The unlucky citizen who drew the short straw and found himself obliged to befriend a member of the shadow cabinet would, at least, have a story to tell.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_5
body: It was one of those campaigns so impeccably well-meaning, so brimful of psychotherapeutic promise, that even the most cynical observer might have hesitated before pointing out where it was all bound to lead. Susie Orbach’s “Antidote” campaign, launched with the aim of bringing “emotional literacy” into British public life, attracted a suitably luminous list of signatories, among them Tessa Jowell, who appended her name to the cause with the same radiant conviction she would later bring to the Olympics. The vision was of a nation in which the stiff upper lip would be replaced by something moister and more expressive, a country where politicians and people could meet in a spirit of mutual vulnerability and growth. One might say, without a trace of irony, that the campaign has succeeded beyond its founders’ wildest dreams. Our politicians now speak, almost without exception, in the language of marriage counsellors, and the public, far from being invited to scrutinise their actions, is addressed as a patient in need of closure. Consider the recent moment when a prime minister, having received an apology from some hapless functionary or foreign leader, pronounced that the gesture allowed everyone to “draw a line and move on”. The phrase, delivered with the practised solemnity of a therapist concluding a particularly unproductive session, was not an isolated slip. Within hours, his press secretary had echoed the sentiment, urging the assembled lobby correspondents to “move on” from the very questions they were trying to ask. The culture secretary, speaking on a morning sofa, volunteered that it was “time for closure” and that the whole affair had been “a learning curve” for all involved. A learning curve, one was given to understand, that was now behind us, its steepness having been conquered by the simple act of the prime minister deciding that it was so. The coordinated deployment of these phrases suggested not so much an administration as a group therapy session for the nation, with the prime minister in the role of the enlightened facilitator, gently but firmly steering the group away from unhelpful dwelling. The thesis, to borrow a term from the consulting room, was that this therapeutic vocabulary was a weapon, not an expression of genuine emotional honesty, and meant nothing more than “I’ve won, so bugger off.” The Patient’s Lament Were one to lie upon the prime minister’s couch, and were that couch not a metaphorical one in the Downing Street den but a real, velveteen-covered chaise, one might find oneself describing feelings of being “stuck”, betrayed, and unable to trust. “I feel,” the citizen-patient might say, “as if I have been constantly told one thing, only to discover that the reality was quite another, and now I am simply expected to forget the whole business.” The prime minister, adjusting his notepad, would nod with the infinite compassion of a man who has himself been on a great many leadership courses. “What I’m hearing,” he might reflect, “is a need for closure. Have you considered, perhaps, a self-help manual?” Should such a patient repair to the high street bookshop, they would find, in the self-help section, a volume perfectly suited to their predicament. Let us imagine it is called *Forgive and Flourish: How to Let Go, Move On, and Reclaim Your Joy*, by the well-regarded therapist Dr Marcia Kindling. The book, with its soothing pastel cover and its comb-binding, would speak directly to the condition of the citizen who has been lied to about weapons of mass destruction, about the costs of a rail project, or about the ease with which a trade deal could be secured. In the chapter entitled “Acknowledging the Hurt”, Dr Kindling would advise the patient to recognise that the person who hurt them is “getting on with their life”, a phrase that, as it happens, could describe the prime minister quite precisely, already launching his next inquiry into something or other, his next “legacy” project, his next appointment to a quango whose acronym will be forgotten within a fortnight. The book would then, in a moment of inspired therapeutic optimism, suggest recalling the “good times” with the perpetrator. One might, as an exercise, make a list. The good times would include, for instance, the promise that there would be no top-down reorganisation of the NHS, the pledge to bring energy bills down, the vow to be the greenest government ever, and the assurance that a certain referendum would be the final word. Each of these good times, held up to the light, takes on the quality of a fondly remembered picnic that turned out, on closer inspection, to have been a mirage. The question that arises, as one wades through Dr Kindling’s exercises, is why politicians should have chosen to appropriate the language of the consulting room in the first place. Real therapists, of the sort who do not appear on daytime television, tend to define “moving on” and “closure” as laborious processes, involving acknowledgement, atonement, and a great deal of discomfort for the person who has done the hurting. They are not declarations that can be handed down from on high, still less imposed on someone else. “Closure” is the most abject of counselling clichés, a word that has been exhausted by American talk shows and the memoirs of former child stars, and it is, by its very nature, a state one arrives at oneself. You cannot claim it on someone else’s behalf, any more than you can declare that a third party has had a good night’s sleep. The prime minister’s version of closure, however, is a unilateral act of administrative efficiency, a sort of emotional clean-up that renders further questions not merely unnecessary but impolite, the conversational equivalent of asking after a dead relative at a drinks party. The Oracle of Impartiality The same therapeutic impulse, the desire to resolve all difficult questions by invoking the authority of a wise and beneficent figure, has found a parallel in the bureaucratic sphere. Consider the culture secretary, who, when asked about the integrity of the process by which the new chairman of the BBC was appointed, has only one answer, repeated with the confidence of a hypnotist: “We have absolute confidence in the independent commissioner’s judgment.” The commissioner in question, a figure of whom we shall hear a great deal, is the government’s chosen guarantor of fairness, a person whose very name, when uttered, is intended to still all further inquiry. Ask who will oversee the overseer, and the culture secretary’s expression suggests you have just asked who will check the homework of God. It is worth pausing to examine the person in whom such boundless trust is placed. The commissioner, let us call him Sir Peregrine Tillingfold, possesses a CV so long and so densely packed with non-executive directorships, honorary professorships, advisory roles, and consulting positions that it resembles a late Victorian novel, full of minor characters who appear once and are never mentioned again. He is, according to his official biography, a board member of the Royal Institute of Good Governance, a visiting fellow at the Centre for Ethical Leadership, a senior adviser to the Council for Transparent Appointments, and the emeritus chairman of the Association of Impartial Oversight. He holds, simultaneously, a fellowship at a Cambridge college, a consultant’s role at a firm of management consultants, and a part-time post as the “fairness champion” for a regulator whose name you have already forgotten. His annual salary for these various roles, should one take the trouble to add them up, is a figure so large that it belongs more comfortably in a discussion of the defence budget. Sir Peregrine’s leadership philosophy, as outlined in his popular management guide *Soaring Together: The Tillingfold Way*, is a treasury of those maxims that sound profound until you write them down. “I fly at 30,000 feet,” he explains, “but I know when to land and walk.” He has compiled a list of “unspoken employee requests” that includes, unforgettably, “acknowledge the greatness within me” and “celebrate my uniqueness before I have to ask.” He has composed a short poem, printed in the book’s foreword, that compares life to a circus, with the reader as “the ringmaster of your own destiny”. And he is an enthusiastic admirer of a famous general, from whom he has borrowed a set of leadership principles that include the maxim, “Don’t let adverse facts stand in the way of a good decision.” It is a phrase that might, one day, be engraved on the door of his office, were it not already the unofficial motto of the entire government. The culture secretary, were one to raise any of this, would remind us that judging the suitability of Sir Peregrine is “entirely a matter” for the independent commissioner, who alone is qualified to oversee the appointments process. And that, of course, is the beauty of the system. The man who has flown at 30,000 feet, who has acknowledged the greatness within, and who has never let an adverse fact obstruct a good decision, will now be the sole judge of whether he has done a good job. The rest of us, the patients on the couch, are invited to move on.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/train/0/train_6
body:
“I know the cynics will sneer,” the Prime Minister said, launching what he called, with that special gift for the resonant cliché that has served him so reliably, the Great Listening. “They will say it’s a PR stunt, a talking shop, a waste of public money. But I say to them: this is a genuine attempt to hear the voice of the people. Nothing is off the table. We want your honest, unvarnished views.” And so, with that pre-emptive dismissal of the sneerers, the website opened for business, and the people, or at any rate that portion of them moved to contribute to government websites, began to speak.

For those of us who had, perhaps, been hoping for something in the nature of a digital Speakers’ Corner, a great cacophony of grievance and demand, the first few hundred contributions to the Great Listening came as something of a surprise. Here was the voice of the people, certainly, but it was a voice so uniformly, so radiantly grateful that one might have been eavesdropping on a meeting of the Politburo’s inner appreciation circle. “I would like to thank the government for the peace and prosperity we all enjoy today,” wrote a Mrs. B. Trelawney of Eastbourne, in a posting that was, within hours, liked by 457 other listeners. “My family remembers the dark days of the previous administration, when we queued for bread and our children went without shoes. Now, thanks to the Prime Minister’s vision, we have never had it so good.” A Mr. D. Cholmondeley of Cheltenham concurred: “Our town has been transformed. The new bypass alone has cut my journey to the golf club by seven minutes. If I might offer a small, constructive suggestion, it would be that the council consider collecting the recycling bins on a Tuesday rather than a Monday, as the current arrangement sometimes clashes with my pilates class.”

It was, one had to admit, a very particular kind of listening. Scroll down the page and the tributes flowed on, as if composed by a single, over-caffeinated speechwriter with a brief to make the present regime sound like a combination of the New Jerusalem and a particularly well-run municipal car park. A contributor identifying himself only as “A Grateful Pensioner, Pinner” praised the “wise and steady leadership” that had, he said, “steered us through choppy waters to a safe harbour”. A Ms. T. Pankhurst of Manchester, meanwhile, offered thanks for the “unprecedented investment in our schools and hospitals”, adding that her only criticism was that “sometimes the nurses seem a little rushed, but that is only because they care so much”. Even the handful of postings that gestured towards the negative were couched in language of such exquisite deference that they read less like complaints than like the kind of helpful feedback a head butler might offer his master on the quality of the silver polish. “Might I respectfully suggest,” wrote a J. Arbuthnot of Tunbridge Wells, “that the government consider a modest review of the regulations governing the height of leylandii hedges in suburban gardens? It is a small thing, I know, but it would mean so much to so many.”

It was, in its way, a masterclass in managed spontaneity. And then, nestled among the encomia, there appeared a contribution that, at first glance, seemed to strike a slightly more historical note. “In the old days, before the Change,” it began, “London was a city of squalor and despair. The streets were filthy, the people half-starved, and crime was rampant. Children worked in the mines and the factories, and the old were left to rot in the workhouse. We have come so far since those terrible times, and we must never, ever forget the debt we owe to those who delivered us.” It was signed, with a pleasing aptness, E. Blair, Wigan. Any moderately attentive reader of mid-20th-century dystopian fiction will have recognised the passage, more or less, as the party history textbook’s account of pre-Revolution London from Orwell’s *1984*. Whether it had been submitted as a joke by some subversive sixth-former, or had been planted by a mischievous moderator as a test of the website’s vaunted openness, or had simply been mistaken by an overworked intern for yet another piece of authentic gratitude, was impossible to say. It stayed up for three days before vanishing, presumably into the memory hole.

The parallel, once spotted, was hard to shake. For what was the Great Listening, if not a digital Potemkin village, a façade of vibrant civic discourse erected to conceal the empty steppe of actual public engagement? The contributions that were permitted through the net—and one had to assume there was a net, for the alternative, that the British public had spontaneously decided to express itself in the idiom of a 1950s *Pravda* editorial, was too depressing to contemplate—were so relentlessly on-message that they made one nostalgic for the days when the only people who wrote to government websites were angry motorists and conspiracy theorists. Here, instead, was a pageant of manufactured contentment, a parade of grateful subjects thanking the government for their daily bread and their bypasses, their schools and their hospitals, while carefully avoiding any mention of the matters that, in the real world, might actually be troubling them.

One searched in vain, for instance, for any contribution that touched upon the war. Or the terrorism threat level. Or the state of the railways. Or the small matter of constitutional reform. Or, indeed, any of the subjects that, in the days before the Great Listening, had been exercising the columnists and the commentariat. It was as if the entire nation had agreed, in a fit of collective amnesia, that the only issues worthy of discussion were the timing of bin collections, the height of hedges, and the precise degree of warmth with which one ought to thank the Prime Minister for his wisdom. The few complaints that did make it through were either so parochial as to be self-satirising—“Why is there no pedestrian crossing outside the post office in Little Snoring?”—or so carefully blame-shifted that they might have been drafted by a government focus group. “If only people would take more responsibility for their own health,” wrote a P. Moggs of Dorking, “we could relieve the pressure on our wonderful NHS.” It was the citizen as scold, the public as its own disciplinarian, and it was, in its way, a more perfect instrument of control than any censor could devise.

Was this, then, the product of active suppression, of some vast, unseen apparatus of digital minders deleting all dissent? Or was it something sadder and more British: a weary public apathy, a sense that contributing to such exercises was about as useful as shouting into a void, and that only the mad, the sycophantic, and the terminally bored would bother? The Great Listening’s own name, with its unmistakable echo of Big Brother—that other, more famous listener who, in Orwell’s fable, watched and heard everything—suggested that, at some level, the cynics had been given their field day from the very start. One could almost hear the sneering from here.

**The Honours Recycling Scheme**

The news that Sir Peregrine Fotheringay-Thomas, the historian and broadcaster, is to return his MBE in protest at the government’s “erosion of civil liberties” will have come as no great shock to anyone who has followed his increasingly excitable columns in the *New Statesman*. What was more interesting, in the interview he gave to explain his decision, was the elaborate care he took to reassure the “little people”—his phrase, not mine—that he meant no disrespect to those for whom such baubles still hold meaning. “I fully understand,” he said, with that particular brand of patrician condescension that passes for humility among the bien-pensant classes, “that for nurses, dinner ladies, community workers—the real heroes of our society—an MBE can be a source of immense pride. I wouldn’t want to take that away from them. But for someone in my position, with a platform, it’s different.”

It was, one had to admit, a masterstroke of inverted snobbery, worthy of Leona Helmsley’s immortal observation that only little people pay taxes. Here, apparently, only little people still care about MBEs. The rest of us, the columnists and the public intellectuals, have moved on to higher things, like writing 800 words about our own exquisite consciences. And yet, one felt, there was something missing from Sir Peregrine’s gesture: a proper infrastructure for the disposal of unwanted honours. At present, the returning of an MBE is a private, almost furtive affair, conducted by registered post and a terse letter to the Cabinet Office. Surely the time has come for a municipal scheme, something along the lines of the recycling bins for batteries and small electricals that now grace every supermarket foyer. A special amnesty bin, perhaps, placed in the foyer of every town hall, where the conscience-stricken could deposit their gongs with a satisfying clunk, knowing that the metal would be melted down and turned into something useful, like a new hip for an actual nurse.

Benjamin Zephaniah, who refused an OBE in 2003 with rather more dignity, might be invited to cut the ribbon. And then, to complete the circle of performative dissent, the government could publish, alongside the twice-yearly honours list, a supplementary list of Returned Honours, complete with the reasons given. “Sir Peregrine Fotheringay-Thomas: returned MBE in protest at the erosion of civil liberties. Also returned: one OBE (Mrs. B. Trelawney, Eastbourne, who had second thoughts after reading a particularly stirring column).” It would, at a stroke, democratise the whole business of elite self-regard, allowing ordinary people who might otherwise never experience the thrill of being noticed by the establishment to, for a fleeting moment, feel as important as a columnist. And that, surely, is what the Great Listening was all about.

author: Catherine Bennett
title: cross_topic_1/validation/0/validation_2
body:
Is the glass half full or half empty? For once, the Prime Minister’s favoured diagnostic question presents something more than a platitudinous choice between managed decline and managed decline. In the matter of the licence for National Digital Channel 6, the quivering vessel is held to the lips of a nation and, whichever way one tilts it, the contents are bilge. On the one side, Sir Peregrine Topcliffe, the self-branding folk-aphorist and quinoa magnate, whose bid for the licence came dressed in so much homespun bombast about “the people’s voice” that one half expected him to arrive at the commission in a smock, clutching a single ear of wheat. On the other, MogulCorp, the omnivorous conglomerate run by Sir Reginald Gristle, a man whose face, in repose, resembles a bull terrier that has just been informed of a shortfall in its dividend. Two suitors, each more objectionable than the other. The Prime Minister, you will recall, is the man who taught us all to think in terms of full and empty glasses. He will, I trust, forgive us if we drain the whole bottle and call for another, because the business of awarding this licence has become a spectacle of such transcendent incompetence that only inebriation can make it tolerable.

Gristle, as it happens, lost. His public response – a press conference at which his lower lip quivered with all the disciplined theatricality of a Victorian melodrama – was a thing of darkly comic wonder. He dabbed at his eyes, his voice cracking, and spoke of a “betrayal of the viewing public” with the febrile intensity of someone attending the death of Little Nell. One expected him to gasp, at any moment, “I am a little tired – let me go to sleep.” The cameras lingered. The nation, briefly, felt something approaching sympathy, before remembering who he was and that his previous contribution to British culture was a reality programme in which members of the public were sealed into a perspex box with only a bucket and a live commentary by Amanda Holden.

Yet before we weep for Gristle, let us recall, if we can bear it, the moral fervour of those days when the present governing party sat on the opposition benches and swore undying enmity to MogulCorp. The Prime Minister himself, then merely a party leader with a line in messianic cadences, stood before conference and denounced Gristle’s “squalid empire of inanity.” He promised, with that familiar crack in his voice, to replace the profit-making model with a non-profit “People’s Platform,” a channel that would belong to “you, the people” – a phrase he deployed with the casual promiscuity of a weather forecaster discussing barometric pressure. The then-Shadow Culture Secretary, a woman whose default expression suggested she had just bitten into an unripe gooseberry, was even more specific. She cited the broadcasting arrangements of Canada, the Netherlands – any country, really, where the state still kept a protective arm around the cathode-ray. “Not one more penny,” she vowed, “will line the pockets of the Gristle machine.” The language was righteous, punitive, exact. One could almost believe they meant it.

The whole shabby episode has its parallels, inevitably, with that other contemporaneous government triumph, the Millennium Wheel. That great rotating affront to the London skyline, conceived in a frenzy of hubris and delivered years late, now stands as a sort of skeletal fable about political vanity. But the Wheel, which at least turns, is a model of uncomplicated execution compared with the Channel 6 licensing saga. The difference is that, with the Wheel, the government merely promised a visionary public project and delivered a rusting embarrassment. With the licence, they had promised, in opposition, to destroy Gristle; in government, their own commission has now been told by a High Court judge that the process by which they attempted to do so was “conspicuously unfair,” “unlawful,” and “an abuse of power.” That is not merely a broken promise – it is a broken promise that will cost the taxpayer, in compensation and reruns, sums that could have built several moderately ambitious Wheels. And yet, in both cases, nobody of any political consequence has been, or ever will be, held accountable. The fiasco is being framed, in a cascade of ministerial briefings, as a sort of meteorological event, a perfect storm of complexity that no reasonable person could have foreseen. It is as if the licence were issued by an unexpected monsoon rather than by a hand-picked commission whose members were selected, one presumes, by a human hand.

What a mysterious hand it was. It is now more than a year since the Culture Secretary appointed the commission, and the question that has never been satisfactorily answered is: on what conceivable basis? The chairman, Sir Archibald Flummery, was a retired diplomat whose principal distinction was having been the ambassador to a country that no longer exists. His knowledge of broadcasting was, by his own admission, limited to an admiration for the shipping forecast and a fondness for reruns of *Dad’s Army*. He was flanked by a former reality-television contestant, a vegan artisan baker, and an academic whose entire published oeuvre consisted of a single monograph on the semiotics of 1970s roller-disco. Not one of them had managed so much as a community radio station, or could, I suspect, reliably operate the timer on a digital recorder. And yet to them fell the task of evaluating the bids for a £300 million public-service contract, an exercise in financial and regulatory scrutiny that would tax the resources of an investment bank.

With only two bidders to consider, the commission announced that neither was acceptable. This was, one might have thought, an inventive form of institutional suicide. But then they took a second, still more startling step. They invited only one bidder – Sir Peregrine Topcliffe – to try again. Gristle’s consortium, they declared, had failed to demonstrate a “sufficient commitment to local storytelling.” Topcliffe, whose business plan consisted largely of a 24-hour channel dedicated to himself, reading his own folksy aphorisms in a selection of whimsical hats, was deemed, by contrast, to be “visionary.” This piece of favouritism was so blatant that it would have most toddlers reeling with sympathetic indignation. Even the very young understand that if you take two biscuits and give one a second chance while declaring the other altogether too crumbly, you are playing a rigged game.

Gristle, a man who has more lawyers on retainer than the Lord Chancellor has ermine, sought judicial review. Mr Justice Teale, a judge known for the almost insulting clarity of his prose, did not linger over niceties. The commission’s process was, he ruled, “conspicuously unfair.” It was “unlawful.” The singling-out of one bidder for preferential treatment was “an abuse of power.” One would have thought, on reading these words, that Sir Archibald Flummery would have been discovered, within the hour, slumped over his desk with a letter of resignation and a modest decanter. But no. The Culture Secretary, still radiating the lemon-sucking rectitude of her opposition days, issued a statement expressing her “full confidence” in the chairman. Sir Archibald himself professed himself bewildered, and spoke of a “disappointing decision.” He clung to his post for six long weeks, a limpet of self-regard, until the weight of what he called “media vilification” finally prised him loose. When he went, he did not apologise; he blamed journalists for creating an “untenable atmosphere.” The remaining commissioners, having evidently mislaid their copies of the Nolan Principles of public life, elected to stay. They are now presided over by a new chair, Dame Velma Tregowan, who has acknowledged with a candour that borders on the surreal that her commission is “deeply discredited,” but who insists there is simply no time to replace them all. So the very same panel, minus its dishonoured head, is to re-run the entire competition, watched over by a battalion of lawyers.

This, in the end, is what the whole saga has become: a matter not for democratic politics but for the law. Gristle, who sues as naturally as other men breathe, had the Treasury Solicitor’s office – which had, abjectly, approved the original unlawful process – squirming in the dock. His own QC, a silk so plush one could upholster a chaise longue with him, sliced through the commission’s reasoning with the effortless cruelty of a surgeon debriding a wound. Now, the final deliberations are to be overseen by a City law firm that has, by a coincidence so exquisite one could weep, advised MogulCorp in the past, as well as Topcliffe’s holding company, and the Department for Culture, Media and Sport. They are, they assure us, managing any possible conflict of interest with the utmost rigour. The politicians have abdicated; the lawyers have taken charge.

Why not abandon the pretence? Given the choice between the political appointees and the brainy barristers, only a sentimentalist would persist with the farce of democratic accountability. The Prime Minister, for all his guff about the people, seems to agree. He has been appointing lords and lawyers to govern at an accelerating pace – peers, QCs, chancery specialists – because, as his own metaphors might put it, a glass half-full of legal training is worth an entire jeroboam of empty frontbench rhetoric. He has not finished yet. By the next election, we may have no ministers at all, merely a rotating panel of judges and a Prime Minister who communicates exclusively through judicial review. It would, at least, be honest. And it would make for much better television than anything Sir Peregrine Topcliffe might have broadcast, even in his most transcendentally visionary hat.

**This article was amended on 15 March to clarify that Sir Reginald Gristle’s tears were, according to his spokesman, a manifestation of “seasonal allergic rhinitis” and not a sign of emotional distress. A spokesperson for the Department for Culture, Media and Sport said: “The government remains committed to delivering a gold-standard licensing process that works for the people.” The Vegan Baker Commissioner has since launched a new line of nut-based cheeses named “Channel 6.”**

**More comment on the Great Licensing Farce:**
- *How Sir Archie Flummery’s grouse moor connections sunk our broadcasting future* (M. White)
- *The Wheel: fifteen years of rust, regret, and the death of ambition* (J. Freedland)
- *Little Nell and the culture of public weeping: Catherine Bennett on the politics of lachrymosity*

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_103
body:
While the British press has been consuming itself with the question of who the Home Secretary is sleeping with, the deadliest conflict since the Second World War has resumed. There is no contest between the public importance of a political sex scandal and the civil war in the eastern Democratic Republic of Congo, but you could spend several lifetimes searching the front pages before you found a story about the latter. In the past few weeks, the Rwandan army has crossed back into the north-eastern reaches of the DRC. Rival Congolese army factions, some of them loyal to Rwanda, have wheeled into new fighting positions. As always, it is civilians who are paying the price: they are being killed, raped, tortured and forced to flee their homes. The International Rescue Committee found that even before the latest fighting, over 1,000 people were dying every day from disease and malnutrition caused by the previous conflict. Nearly half of them were children under five. This is not a crisis; it is a continuous, rolling slaughter, and our indifference to it is a moral scandal of the first order.

There is a reason for this silence, and it lies in the psychological architecture of a narrative the West has found too comforting to abandon. The story we have told ourselves about Rwanda is one of uncomplicated victimhood. In 1994, as the world shamefully looked away, Hutu Interahamwe militias murdered 800,000 Tutsis and moderate Hutus in a hundred days of machete slaughter. When Paul Kagame’s Rwandan Patriotic Front swept the killers from power, it was not merely a military victory; it was a moral one. The victims had become the righteous avengers. The problem is that this story, frozen in amber, has for years been obscuring a darker and far more complex reality. The Rwandan government, once the world’s most conspicuous survivor of genocide, has become one of its most prolific perpetrators of war crimes and resource plunder.

Rwanda’s initial invasions of what was then Zaire had a powerful justification. The génocidaires had fled across the border, where they were sheltered first by the collapsing regime of Mobutu Sese Seko and then by his successor, Laurent Kabila. From sprawling refugee camps, they planned to regroup and reinvade Rwanda. No sovereign state could be expected to tolerate such a threat. But the mission mutated with extraordinary speed. After the second invasion in 1998, the pretence of hunting the Interahamwe was quietly abandoned and replaced by a systematic project of industrial-scale looting. A United Nations Security Council report found that by 1999, a staggering 80 percent of Rwanda’s military budget – some $320 million a year – was being generated not by the domestic economy, but by the theft of Congolese minerals. 

What unfolded next was a six-nation African turf war fought almost entirely over the mines. In the eastern DRC, millions of people were displaced, and thousands were forced into mining or prostitution at the barrel of a gun. The Rwandan operation, as Amnesty International documented, was the most ruthlessly efficient of all. It was not a chaotic scramble by undisciplined soldiers; it was directed from the heart of the state in Kigali. A dedicated “Congo Desk” was established within the Rwandan army to manage the extraction and marketing of diamonds and coltan – the ore used in mobile phones. Even after the official withdrawal of foreign troops in 2002, the plunder continued, sustained by proxy militia groups armed and financed by Rwanda. The latest invasion is a thinly disguised effort to neutralise those who now threaten this lucrative network.

Why, then, does this barely register in the Western conscience? We have been transfixed by the horrors of Darfur, and rightly so, but the scale of death in the Congo is an order of magnitude greater. The difference is that in Darfur we can identify a clear narrative of Arab janjaweed aggressors against African victims, a simple binary that fits our moral categories. In the Congo, the aggressor is Rwanda, and Rwanda is staffed by people we are accustomed to seeing as the “good guys.” The psychology is as pervasive as it is dangerous: we find it almost impossible to accept that the victims of atrocity can themselves become perpetrators, and some of the bloodiest war criminals of our age. The cognitive dissonance is too great, so we resolve it by looking away.

To criticise the Kagame government, one is often told, is to play into the hands of the very powers on the UN Security Council who failed to stop the genocide. This is a sophisticated form of emotional blackmail, and it collapses under the weight of evidence. The organisations that have reached the same damning conclusions about Rwanda’s activities are not French neo-colonialists or American hawks. They are Amnesty International, Human Rights Watch, the International Crisis Group, Global Witness, the British all-party parliamentary group on the Great Lakes, and the US State Department. When such a politically disparate chorus sings the same grim tune, the claim of a geopolitical stitch-up evaporates.

The reports they have produced run to hundreds of pages. They are filled with eyewitness accounts, testimony from survivors and confessions from perpetrators. They describe children as young as three being raped, and women who resisted having their genitals mutilated. They recount how forced labourers in the mines were buried alive in cave-ins. They detail villages being torched, possessions looted, and anyone who protested being killed. Millions were driven into the jungle, where they died, unrecorded and unremarked, of malnutrition and disease. These deaths are no less a crime than those inflicted by a bullet. The documentary record is forensically precise: the names of the agents, the identities of the victims, the dates, the grid references, the market value of the stolen resources, and, most incriminating of all, the names of the Western companies that bought them.

A defence sometimes mounted by apologists for the regime is that we must not confuse the disciplined Rwandan army with the chaotic rebel groups operating in the region. The journalist Victoria Brittain, for example, has made this argument. It is a distinction without a moral difference. The US State Department has summarised “numerous credible reports” of regular Rwandan troops killing, torturing and raping civilians in the provinces of North and South Kivu and Maniema. In any case, there is no ethical distinction between pulling the trigger and commissioning a proxy to do it. The RCD-Goma militia, which has terrorised eastern Congo for years, was not a spontaneous local uprising. It was created in Kigali, financed by the Rwandan state, and equipped with heavy weapons. Both Global Witness and Amnesty International have traced the chain of command directly to the Rwandan ministry of defence.

The old excuse of the Interahamwe – that Rwanda’s activities are a necessary and permanent hunt for the génocidaires – has worn dangerously thin. The government in Kinshasa is itself guilty of terrible crimes, but its authority barely runs in the east, where an entirely different set of forces holds sway. A UN report leaked to the BBC confirmed that Rwanda and its proxies are the dominant military and economic power in the region, controlling the bulk of the mineral trade and involved in almost every cycle of violence. More fatally for the official narrative, a report by the International Crisis Group noted that Rwanda enjoyed total military control of eastern Congo from 1996 to 2002, and yet it failed to neutralise the Interahamwe. In fact, the British parliamentary group found that Rwandan forces were frequently stationed in areas where no Interahamwe existed, and on several occasions even allied with them to secure control of the mines. The past is being used as a cynical cloak for the present, an old excuse dragged out to justify a new war of conquest.

This is not a tragedy beyond the reach of the international community. Rwanda is a tiny, fragile state, overwhelmingly dependent on foreign aid. Over a third of its external assistance comes from Britain alone. A quiet word from a major donor, a threat to suspend budget support unless the army withdraws and the mineral racket stops, would have an immediate and transformative effect. The levers exist. They are not being pulled. Until we collectively wake up to the reality of this war, until we stop pretending that the victims of an atrocious crime cannot also become its perpetrators, the slaughter in the forests of the Congo will continue, and the bodies will continue to pile up in a silence that shames us all.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_104
body:
It has become a comforting orthodoxy among many on the left that the administration of George W. Bush represents a form of fascism, either nascent or fully-blown. Writers like Barbara Probst Solomon have made the case explicitly, and Richard Sennett has traced the lineaments of a "soft" or "velvet" fascism in the American project. The notion circulates widely on the internet, a satisfying shorthand for everything we despise. It is also profoundly wrong, and its wrongness matters. By misdiagnosing the disease, we prescribe the wrong cure.

Let us apply the definition of fascism proposed by its most careful anatomist, Robert Paxton. In *The Anatomy of Fascism*, he describes it not as a fixed set of beliefs but as a form of political behaviour. It is, he argues, a politics of passion rooted in a sense of overwhelming community crisis. A nation feels itself humiliated, in decline, a victim of internal and external enemies. From this ferment arises a mass movement of hysterical nationalism, a cult of unity, energy and purity, all directed by a party that collaborates uneasily with traditional elites but draws its power and momentum from an angry popular mobilization. Fascism, in Paxton's framing, is driven from below and then harnessed by the top; it is the frenzied marching of a wounded people seeking a compensatory greatness.

Little of this fits the Bush project. The administration's power does not arise from hysterical popular mobilization. The public was not screaming for the invasion of Iraq; it had to be seduced, frightened and deceived into submission. There were no mass rallies of the party faithful demanding the purification of the state. The cult of unity was manufactured by press secretaries, not forged in street battles. Bush's people did not arise from a sense of national humiliation—the American wound of 9/11 was not one of historical decline but of violated omnipotence—and they did not depend on a mobilized populace. They governed through the supine compliance of a traumatized nation, not its violent engagement. To call this fascism is to misunderstand the nature of the beast, and as a result, to fail to see that the Bush administration was not a novel eruption but the revival of a quite different and much older ideology. To oppose it effectively, we must name it correctly: this was, in essence, a revival of Puritanism.

We misunderstand Puritanism if we imagine it only as a joyless cult of self-denial, a carnival of church-stripping, theatre-closing and entertainment-banning. This caricature, accurate enough in its details, fails to explain why such a grim creed could take root and flourish. It seems distant from the conspicuous consumption of Republican America, the televangelist bling and the corporate tax cut. But Puritanism was not merely a psychological affliction; it was the ideological product of a massive economic transformation, and it is in that transformation that its true nature—and its modern recurrence—must be sought.

In the early 17th century, England still retained the remnants of what we might call the feudal state. This was a regulatory apparatus that, in the terms proposed by R.H. Tawney in his magnificent 1926 study *Religion and the Rise of Capitalism*, functioned analogously to the social democracy of the 20th century. It was hierarchical, serving the interests of monarchy and clergy, but it also sought to restrain economic exploitation. The state did not merely extract; it regulated. Charles I, as Tawney documented, pursued policies that would now be called interventionist: he sought to nationalize certain industries, to control foreign exchange, and to prosecute lords who evicted peasants, employers who underpaid workers, and magistrates who denied poor relief. The law, however imperfectly, held a space for the idea of social obligation.

This model collapsed under the weight of a revolution in economic power. Over some 150 years, the great international commercial companies concentrated financial power on a scale hitherto unknown. Commerce and finance, not industry, drove the transformation. Medieval forms of industrial organization were subjected to a new, liquid, rootless money-power. Monarchs, once the arbiters of economic life, became the puppets of financiers. In England, the dissolution of the monasteries served as the great catalyst, triggering a massive seizure of wealth by a rising commercial class. Land was enclosed, peasants were evicted, and speculation ran riot. A sophisticated financial market emerged, experimenting in futures and arbitrage, and perfecting all the vices we have come to associate with the Age of Enron. The new rich were not builders but traders; their wealth was not made from tangible production but from the manipulation of paper and property.

The early Reformation theologians responded to this exploitation with righteous fury. They preached charity, justice, and the divine punishment of oppressors. This spirit persisted among some of the New England settlers, but in England itself it was crushed by the very forces it sought to tame. Puritanism, which began as a protest against the moral laxity of the old church, was captured and remade by the new commercial classes: the traders, money-lenders, bankers and industrialists. They found in Calvin's doctrines what the old order could not provide: a powerful theological justification for commerce. Capitalism, in this new reading, could glorify God.

The ideological inversion was a work of terrible genius. From Calvin's doctrine of individual purification there emerged an idealization of personal responsibility before God. This, in turn, became a theory of individual rights, one that stood the traditional scheme of Christian virtues on its head. By the mid-17th century, most English Puritans had come to see poverty not as a misfortune or a social problem, but as a sign of moral failure. Wealth, conversely, was a blessing, the reward of energy and will—a visible token of spiritual grace. Individualism in religion bred an individualist morality, which led inexorably to a disparagement of the social fabric. Communal obligation was a relic.

The Puritans then performed their masterstroke: they conflated the religious calling with the commercial one. Listen to the preacher Richard Steele in 1684, addressing the tradesman: "Be diligent in your calling, and keep your shop, and your shop will keep you... You are serving the Lord Christ as well in your shop as in the closet." Business success became the proof of election. A century before Adam Smith, Joseph Lee anticipated the invisible hand with a brutal candour: the advancement of private persons, he claimed, serves the public good. In practice, this meant that the advancement of men of property required the destruction of everyone else's.

This new theology birthed what Tawney identified as an early form of "administrative nihilism": the fervent belief in the minimal state. Business affairs, the Puritans argued, should be settled by businessmen, unhampered by antiquated moral scruples. They formulated a new theory of social obligation: that to help the poor was to create idleness and spiritual dissolution. Charity did not alleviate suffering; it perpetuated sin. There is, admittedly, one signal difference from the modern American right. The Puritans worshipped production, not consumption. Their world was to be conquered, not enjoyed. But this is the same disease with a different symptom, for the logic is identical. In Tawney's chilling summary, the world exists not to be savoured but to be subdued, and only its conqueror deserves the name of Christian—or, in the modern idiom, of American.

The Puritans did not go unopposed. The Levellers and the Diggers, who clung to the original spirit of the Reformation, preached a radical egalitarianism. They were violently suppressed, their voices erased from history. The masses, brutalized by enclosure and impoverished by speculation, were offered a moral distraction: the pursuit of adulterers, sodomites, and other deviants. The parallels are not merely academic. In his novel *Havoc in its Third Year*, set in the 1630s, Ronan Bennett produced an almost unbearable parable of our own time. His Puritan magistrates are obsessed with terrorists—Irish and Jesuit agents—and with homosexuality and sexual license. They mete out vicious punishment to moral deviants while disparaging public support for the poor. Swap their black suits for the grey flannel of the Bush functionary, and the characters could walk straight out of Dick Cheney's Washington. The moral panic, the punitive cruelty, the demonization of the poor, and the sanctification of wealth: all are present.

Why, then, has this ideology resurfaced in 2004? The answer is as grim as it is simple: because it must. The current enrichment of the elite and the simultaneous immiseration of the lower classes cannot be sustained without a powerful justifying ideology, a moral framework that transforms exploitation into virtue. In the United States, that ideology must be religious; no other language could cloak such rapacity in such righteousness. The Bush administration was not a freakish accident but a historical reflex. Driven by necessity, it reached back not to the 1930s but to the 1630s. To understand what we are confronting, we must learn to recognize the old enemy in its new clothes. The road to the White House does not lead through Nuremberg, but through the purged churches and enclosed fields of early modern England.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_105
body:
The agency head’s impossible role:
To give away bombs while saving his soul.
He hands out the matches,
Then curses the catches—
And wonders why he’s lost control.

This, in essence, is the task assigned to Mohamed ElBaradei and his predecessor Hans Blix: to prevent the proliferation of nuclear weapons while disseminating the materials and expertise required to build them. It is a contradiction so glaring that it has lately permitted Iran to dismiss the IAEA’s demands as the meddling of an agency that, by its own statute, is obliged to help it develop the very capabilities it is now being asked to suspend. The agency is tasked with taking away nuclear weapons capability with one hand while distributing the means to build it with the other. It is a circus act in which the juggler is also the arsonist.

The contradiction was born in a moment of genuine optimism. On 8 December 1953, President Eisenhower addressed the United Nations and unveiled his “Atoms for Peace” plan. A newspaper of the time predicted that nuclear power would soon propel “atomic cars”, end coal mining, and usher in an “earthly paradise”. Eisenhower spoke of beating “the nuclear sword into a ploughshare” and promised to distribute fissionable materials to “power-starved” regions as an incentive to forswear weapons. The IAEA, founded four years later, was given a dual mandate: to prevent the spread of nuclear weapons and to accelerate the spread of nuclear technology. Its statute enjoins it to “seek to accelerate and enlarge the contribution of atomic energy to peace, health and prosperity” while ensuring that assistance “is not used in such a way as to further any military purpose”. The Non-Proliferation Treaty of 1970 codified this double purpose, granting non-nuclear states the “inalienable right” to peaceful nuclear energy in return for a pledge not to seek bombs. It made perfect sense—as long as the miracle remained miraculous.

This programme made sense only while the dream was still dreamtable. In the past 30 years, every state that has developed nuclear weapons—Israel, South Africa, India, Pakistan, North Korea, and Iraq—has done so by diverting resources from a civilian nuclear programme, often with direct Atoms for Peace assistance. India’s bomb was built with reactors and heavy water supplied by Canada, the United States, Germany, France, Norway, and the United Kingdom. Pakistan received parallel assistance, its bomb built with centrifuge designs stolen from the Netherlands and components from Germany and the UK. The line between civilian and military nuclear programmes is not blurred; it is nonexistent. South Africa developed its weapons under the cover of a civilian programme, with help from the US and Germany. Israel’s Dimona reactor was supplied by France. North Korea’s programme began with a Soviet-supplied reactor. Iraq’s Osirak reactor was built by France. In every case, the atoms provided for peace were forged into weapons. Today, roughly 20 countries possess the latent capacity to become weapons states within months, thanks almost entirely to foreign civilian nuclear assistance. Russia, far from being permitted to supply Iran with uranium and bomb-making technology, was obliged to do so under the NPT. The treaty that was supposed to stop proliferation has become its principal engine. It is as if we had passed a law requiring the police to distribute firearms to anyone who asked, on the understanding that they would only be used for target practice.

Nowhere is the contradiction more glaring than in Iran. The Iranians are enriching uranium and producing heavy water—processes that are entirely legitimate for power generation but can be repurposed for weapons with scarcely a pause. Enriching uranium to bomb-grade requires only a few additional passes through the same centrifuges; the heavy water reactor at Arak could produce plutonium. The NPT gives Iran not only the materials but the legal cover: if you want a bomb, you sign the treaties, join the IAEA, and use your entitlements. And Iran has every motive to want one. It sees Israel’s unacknowledged nuclear arsenal, protected by a United States that refuses to pressure its ally to sign the NPT. It sees the US and the UK abandoning their own disarmament obligations, developing new tactical nukes, and asserting the right to pre-emptive use. It sees American military bases on every side. And it has absorbed the lesson of North Korea: that possessing the bomb grants impunity, while lacking it invites invasion. Saddam Hussein had no nuclear weapons; he was toppled. Kim Jong-il has them; he is courted. If Iran is not seeking nuclear weapons, it has profoundly misunderstood the nature of global politics. It would be the first nation in history to possess the means, the motive, and the legal cover, and yet choose to remain defenceless. That is not a reasonable expectation; it is a fantasy.

ElBaradei’s helplessness is the direct result of the contradiction he embodies. He can only beg Iran to accept “voluntary agreements”—the Additional Protocol, a suspension of enrichment—because the treaty he is charged with enforcing gives him no authority to stop legally permitted activities. He is a firefighter who is required to hand out matches. The danger is no longer theoretical. The official nuclear powers have junked the NPT—the US and UK by modernising their arsenals and threatening first use, France by testing new warheads, Russia and China by expanding their capabilities—while the non-nuclear powers exploit its loopholes. The spectre now is of a Middle Eastern nuclear cascade. A nuclear-armed Iran would not exist in isolation; Saudi Arabia, Egypt, and Turkey would swiftly follow. The Middle East would become a nuclear tinderbox, and a spark from Hezbollah could ignite an Israeli strike on Iran, pulling in the rest. The world is being prepared for a war that ElBaradei’s agency is simultaneously trying to prevent and, by its very existence, helping to cause. He is like a man trying to empty a sinking boat with a bucket while his other hand is drilling holes in the hull.

The obvious but unspoken conclusion is that you cannot phase out nuclear weapons without phasing out nuclear power. As long as the technology exists, the temptation and the means will exist alongside it. We need a new nuclear treaty, one based on the grim realities of proliferation rather than the utopian fantasy of a benign atomic age. Eisenhower’s ploughshare has been reforged into a sword, and the IAEA is holding both ends. The agency’s founding fathers believed they could separate the atom’s peaceful and destructive faces. They were wrong. The two are fused at the molecular level. To pursue one is to enable the other. There is no middle ground, no moderate position worth taking. We must choose: either we accept the spread of nuclear weapons as the price of nuclear power, or we abandon both. The agency that was supposed to save us has instead become the most effective instrument of proliferation the world has ever seen. Its head, ElBaradei, is not a peacemaker; he is a high priest of a cult that demands human sacrifice. He is, in the most literal sense, destroying the world in order to save it. And we are the offerings.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/test/1/test_106
body:
Why does a nation overflowing with brilliance, ingenuity, and restless energy so reliably find itself, every four years, forced to choose between two deeply unimpressive men? The answer is blunt and circular: because unimpressive men keep getting elected. And here we are again, in 2004, with the progressive establishment once more instructing its voters to swallow hard, hold their noses, and choose the safer, duller option. We are told, with a gravity that brooks no dissent, that this is no ordinary election, that the circumstances are exceptional, that the stakes are too high for principle.

Let us lay out the progressive establishment's case honestly, and with some sympathy, for it is made by people of genuine intelligence and commitment. Nearly every major progressive voice—the Guardian, the Nation magazine, even Noam Chomsky—has urged voters in swing states to back John Kerry. The logic is seductive in its simplicity. Kerry may be about as inspiring as a parking lot on a wet Sunday in Detroit, a man whose campaign seems to have been designed by a committee of actuaries, but he is the only vessel into which the anti-Bush vote can be poured. The race is so tight, they warn, that a few thousand votes for Ralph Nader in Ohio or Florida could tip the entire election to George W. Bush. The Republicans, with their usual grubby genius, have even been caught funneling money to Nader’s campaign to ensure he stays on the ballot and plays the spoiler. The argument has real merit; to pretend otherwise is to be glib about the machinery of power.

And what a catastrophe a second Bush term would be. Let us enumerate the offenses, for they are staggering. He has launched two unnecessary wars, one of them on a false prospectus, and now threatens military action against dozens of nations. He has shredded international treaties and domestic regulations with a vandal’s glee, granting corporations license to accelerate climate change while dismantling the very idea that the state should tax the rich to help the poor. He has orchestrated a global assault on civil liberties, from Guantánamo to the Patriot Act, and in doing so has trashed the Enlightenment principles that once, however imperfectly, guided American statecraft. The world would be palpably safer without him. To hand this man four more years through a fit of progressive pique seems, to many, an act of criminal self-indulgence.

Progressive outlets have made this “exceptional circumstances” argument with increasing desperation. A Guardian leader intoned that Kerry’s flaws, while real, are overshadowed by Bush’s neoconservative agenda and catastrophic war-making. The Nation published an open letter to Ralph Nader, signed by figures of immense moral stature, asserting that 2004 is not 2000, that the threat posed by Bush is of a different order, and that his defeat is a political imperative that must override all other considerations. These are not stupid people. They are scared people, and they have good reason to be.

But here is the counter-punch, and it is devastating in its simplicity: both publications said precisely the same thing in 2000. The Nation’s Eric Alterman, in a spasm of retrospective fury, blamed Al Gore’s defeat squarely on Nader’s “megalomania,” arguing that the progressive cause was set back a generation by his refusal to stand down. And yet, before that election, the Guardian ran a piece calling Gore the “safe, wise choice,” a man who was neither exciting nor visionary but who would, at least, not be George W. Bush. The pattern extends backwards almost indefinitely. Similar warnings, couched in almost identical language, appear in the archives before every modern presidential election. The circumstances are always exceptional. The Republican is always a unique menace. The Democrat is always a disappointment, but the only alternative.

The reason for this grim recurrence is structural. The US electoral system, built on a foundation of patronage, corporate corruption, and a media that sets the boundaries of acceptable debate with iron discipline, will always produce dreadful candidates. It is a machine designed to extrude men like John Kerry: cautious, compromised, and utterly dependent on the forces that made him. Because the system reliably generates such figures, it also reliably generates the “exceptional circumstances” that are used to bludgeon dissenters into line. Only a leader willing to overturn the system itself, to reintroduce democracy to a hollowed-out republic, will ever break this cycle. Voting for the bad to stop the terrible in 2004 guarantees precisely the same dilemma in 2008, and in 2012, and onward into a future of diminishing returns and accumulating disasters.

Such systemic change requires political courage of a kind that is almost extinct. It means taking on the corporations that buy elections and the media conglomerates that police the limits of thought. Here, the contrast with John Kerry is almost too painful to draw. Kerry displayed genuine physical courage in Vietnam, a fact that his campaign has used as a flak jacket against criticism. But on the campaign trail, courage has been entirely absent. He has flinched from every fight that matters. He stated, with a candor that must have made his strategists weep, that “even knowing what we now know,” he would still have voted to authorize the Iraq war. His national security adviser, James Rubin, told the Washington Post that Kerry would “in all probability” have attacked Iraq by now if he were president. This is not a man who will challenge the imperial consensus; this is a man who will manage it with slightly more polish.

Kerry’s prodigious fundraising, which his supporters tout as a sign of democratic vitality, is better understood as corporate investment. His backers are not writing checks out of civic virtue; they are purchasing compliance, and they are unlikely to be disappointed. When Kerry gets his orders, he reports for duty. The notion that such a man could oversee democratic reform is laughable.
