He is a frightened and flinching politician, one who made the system work for him by working for the system, and he knows it would destroy him if he ever turned against it. He lacks the charisma of a Hugo Chávez, the popular enthusiasm that might allow a leader to break free of elite control. A vote for Kerry is not merely a vote against Bush; it is a vote for the survival of the system that made Bush possible, a system that will, with industrial regularity, produce further Bushes.

Now pivot to Ralph Nader. I have my disagreements with him—on trade, for instance, his positions sometimes seem to ignore the complexities of global poverty. But no one with a shred of intellectual honesty can deny his courage. His decision to run this year, in the face of near-universal progressive condemnation and a Republican-funded smear campaign, is both brave and foolhardy. Nader has spent a career fighting corporations, enduring media attacks that would have broken a less obstinate man. He has been willing to take on the Anti-Defamation League, which he accuses of smearing critics of Israeli policy as antisemites—a taboo that no other US politician has dared to touch. This is not the behavior of a megalomaniac; it is the behavior of someone who has decided that the game is rigged and must be exposed.

Nader will not win. Let us concede that immediately. But the point is not to win this election; the point is that systemic change must begin now, not in some endlessly postponed future when the circumstances are finally, magically, unexceptional. The better Nader performs, the faster the movement for change will grow. The Nation has suggested that Nader would have more impact as a “public citizen,” advocating for open debates and rallying voters behind progressive Democrats. But what incentive would Democrats have to listen to him then? He holds leverage over these cowering creatures only while they fear he will take their votes. The moment he stands down, he is irrelevant to them, a scold to be ignored.

I acknowledge that there are differences between Kerry and Bush. Bush is active in his malice, Kerry passive. Kerry wants high defense spending but would be more reluctant to attack other nations. He wants tax cuts but is less willing to gut health and education. He wants lower oil prices but won’t help corporations open the Arctic Wildlife Refuge. These are not nothing. But is this difference enough to justify abandoning the only current electoral effort to democratize US politics? The answer, when the question is stripped of panic, is no. The progressive establishment is asking voters to trade liberty for security and democracy for expediency. It is a plea that should be recognized for what it is, and firmly ignored.

author: George Monbiot
title: cross_topic_1/validation/1/validation_5
body:
The story, as it first emerged, possessed the reassuringly narrow dimensions of a classic British scandal. A planning minister, Nick Raynsford, was accused of a cover-up. The charge was that he had personally intervened to allow a listed building in Oxford to be bulldozed and moved, clearing the way for a new business school, funded in part by the billionaire arms broker Wafic Said. It had the familiar whiff of money talking and heritage walking. But then, just as the outrage was beginning to simmer, the minister’s office produced an alibi of apparently cast-iron solidity. On the very day the fateful decision was made, Raynsford was not hunched over a desk in Whitehall, whispering into a developer’s ear. He was in Egypt, leading a government trade mission. He could not have been the villain, the story went, because he was busy being a statesman. The case, it seemed, was closed before it had even opened.

But this alibi, so deftly deployed, does not close the case. It blows it wide open. For the truly disturbing question is not whether Raynsford personally signed the death warrant for that Oxford building. It is what on earth a planning minister was doing leading a trade delegation to Egypt in the first place. The alibi is not an exoneration; it is an indictment. It reveals a structural corruption so profound that the alleged original crime, significant as it is, dwindles to a mere symptom. We have been invited to peer at a crack in the plaster while the foundations of the house are being devoured by rot.

The delegation Raynsford led was a floating bazaar of 27 British corporations, a Who’s Who of the development-industrial complex: Balfour Beatty, Enron, Laing, Mott MacDonald, Thames Water, WS Atkins. They had descended on Egypt to sniff around one of the world’s most contentious megaprojects, the Toshka scheme. This is a $90 billion plan to gouge a new river valley from the desert, diverting 10% of the Nile’s flow into a colossal network of canals and industrial farms. It has been condemned in almost equal measure by environmentalists, who see it as an ecological suicide note for the Nile Delta, and by hard-headed financial consultants, who see it as a hallucinatory boondoggle destined to evaporate both water and money. It is, in other words, precisely the kind of pharaonic, landscape-annihilating folly that, if proposed in the Home Counties, would fall under the remorseless regulatory scrutiny of the planning minister. Raynsford’s job at home is to police this sort of thing. Abroad, he was acting as its cheerleader-in-chief.

The mission’s most instructive detail, however, is not who was on the plane, but who won the prize. The major contract for Toshka’s pumping station did not go to a British firm. It was captured by the Norwegian engineering giant Kvaerner. This might seem like a simple failure of the boosterism Raynsford was sent to practise, until one examines Kvaerner’s relationship with the British state. It had, at the time, seconded more employees into the Department of the Environment, Transport and the Regions than any other company. This is the soft, creeping tendril of corporate capture, the process by which the regulator’s brain is quietly rewired to think like the regulated. The British firms didn’t need to win the contract directly. Their interests were already embedded within the ministry, shaping its very conception of the possible.

This brings us to the grotesque structural conflict at the heart of Raynsford’s ministerial brief. He was, simultaneously, the minister for planning and the minister for construction. He was both the poacher and the gamekeeper, the regulator of development and its official promoter within government. This is not a tension he has sought to resolve with any semblance of impartiality. It is a conflict he has resolved, routinely and systematically, in favour of the industry whose interests he is supposed to police. He told *Building* magazine, with a candour that is almost admirable in its brazenness, that his job was “creating a climate in which the industry can do well.” Not a climate in which the public interest is defended, or the environment protected, or local democracy respected. A climate for business. The minister had become a missionary.

The pattern of favouritism is as relentless as it is damning. When the quarrying industry was threatened by the prospect of a tax on virgin aggregates—a levy designed to curb the relentless disfigurement of the countryside—Raynsford’s department did not analyse the policy’s merits. It sponsored a public relations campaign, sickeningly christened “Minerals 98,” to help the firms lobby against it. At Labour’s 1998 local government conference, he took to the stage to argue against taxes on out-of-town parking, a policy his own government was promoting to save city centres from the retail blight of the car. He promised the British Council of Shopping Centres that he would accelerate planning decisions on retail schemes. And when faced with a proposal to make new homes more energy efficient, a measure that would save money and carbon in equal measure, he dismissed it as “an undue burden on builders.” In every case, the public good was sacrificed on the altar of private convenience.

Yet the gravest allegation, the one that lifts this story from the grubby register of routine corporate welfare into the realm of the truly sinister, concerns not a shopping centre or a gravel pit, but a dam. The Ilisu dam in Turkey, a concrete monster poised to drown the ancient city of Hasankeyf and forcibly displace up to 78,000 Kurdish people, is an act of cultural annihilation and ethnic cleansing by water. The British government, after initial hesitation, announced it was “minded to grant” £200 million in export credit guarantees for the scheme, handing a colossal public subsidy to the British firms—Balfour Beatty prominent among them—hoping to build it. The chronology is chilling. In September 1999, Raynsford led another trade mission, this time to Turkey. He was accompanying a consortium fronted by Balfour Beatty, which was hungrily seeking a slice of the £4 billion in contracts for earthquake reconstruction. Balfour Beatty also happens to be the lead contractor hoping to build the Ilisu dam. Three months after that mission, the government’s damascene conversion on the dam was complete. The question that hangs, unanswered, in the air is a simple one: were British reconstruction contracts made conditional on British support for a project of mass destruction? Was the fate of the Kurds bartered in a hotel conference room for a slice of seismic spoils?

And so we return, by a circular route, to the Oxford business school. The real scandal is not that Nick Raynsford might have bent a rule to help a wealthy benefactor. It is that everything he says about the decision is probably true. There was, in all likelihood, nothing unusual about it. The outrage is not a single, covert act of vandalism. It is that turning a blind eye to the destruction of heritage and environment on behalf of the construction industry is not a secret crime requiring an alibi. It is the weekly, unremarkable business of the very man appointed to stop it. The alibi is the crime.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_119
body:
I have been studying America for more than thirty years, and coming here for nearly as long. I have written books about it, filed dispatches from its every corner, married into it, educated my children in its schools. I thought I knew the place. But I did not know it. The shock of September 11 was not merely the shock of the atrocity itself; it was the shock of discovering that the country I had so assiduously observed had been, in some essential way, opaque to me. Perhaps, in truth, America did not fully know itself until that morning. The Afghan war, the anthrax letters, the extraordinary collective introspection that followed the collapse of the towers—these forced a national self-examination that had not occurred since perhaps the Civil War. What it has revealed is not that America has changed. It is, rather, that America has become *more so*. Each of its defining national traits, the virtues and the vices, has been intensified. The ambiguity has been burned away.

I write this from a remote corner of Vermont, where my family and I have come to spend Christmas. It is a liberal redoubt, a place of covered bridges and clapboard houses, of Bernie Sanders, the self-described socialist who represents the state in Congress. The snow lies deep and the nights are long. It is, one might think, an unlikely vantage point from which to generalize about a nation of 280 million souls. Yet it is precisely from such a small, particular place that the larger truth sometimes becomes visible.

Consider, first, the matter of consideration itself. Americans have always been a considerate people; the casual kindness of the stranger is one of the things that most strikes the European visitor. But since September 11, this has deepened into something almost preternatural. People are super-caring. The attack was not experienced as a blow against a distant government or a symbolic edifice; it was a shared wound, inflicted on the collective body. Three months later, the shock still reverberates in daily encounters. There has been no stoical sloughing-off, no brisk national compact to move on and put it behind us. Instead, one meets people who are still blundering about, searching for meaning, and their instinctive response is to care more tenderly for those around them. The man who serves you coffee asks after your family with an earnestness that is not merely formulaic. The neighbour who has never before spoken of anything weightier than the weather now wants to talk about the state of your soul. It is, by turns, touching and exhausting, but it is utterly authentic.

Then there is the self-confidence. This is the trait that must madden Osama bin Laden more than any other. There is no whining here, no self-pity. Self-sufficiency is a second nature so ingrained that even a catastrophic blow cannot dislodge it. The American certainties—about the rightness of the country's cause, about the efficacy of its methods—have not weakened. They have redoubled. One small but telling sign is the return of humour. For a period after the attacks, the national mood was too solemn for irony. But now the jokes have crept back, and they are pointed. I heard Garrison Keillor, the bard of Lake Wobegon, deliver a monologue on "jihad" the other evening, pulling the word gently to pieces, domesticating it with prairie wit. The audience laughed, not nervously but with a kind of relieved recognition. Islam, it seems, has been silently dropped from the unwritten list of things protected by political correctness. The right to mock is a declaration of confidence.

Patriotism is the most visible of the intensified traits. Flags have always hung from Vermont porches; they are not an invention of the present crisis. But now they are everywhere, reproduced on cars and lapels and shopfronts with an earnestness that borders on the devotional. The irony, of course, is that this is a region which sends a socialist to Congress and which holds George W. Bush in something close to contempt. Yet the patriotic response to September 11 is unquestioning. It is not a matter of partisan allegiance. It is something deeper, more tribal. Here in the snow, we read the *Valley News* of White River Junction, a paper of resolutely local habit. Its front page now carries the swearing-in of Hamid Karzai in Kabul and the news that Yasser Arafat has been permitted to visit Bethlehem. This is not a fresh burst of internationalism; it is a shedding of the old, incurious indifference toward the world from which the threat emanated. America's role is simply to protect America. And that, to the people here, is nothing to be ashamed of.

On the matter of constitutionalism, however, the change is more marked, and more troubling. I have talked to friends here, good liberals all, about the military tribunals and the quiet curtailments of due process that the Bush administration has introduced. Their objections are modest, almost apologetic. The administration has been adept at citing precedent: Lincoln suspended habeas corpus during the Civil War; Franklin Roosevelt interned Japanese-Americans during the Second World War. In times of existential threat, the argument runs, the Constitution is not a suicide pact. This seems to satisfy people. I find it chilling, but I am a foreigner, and perhaps there is something in the American grain that accepts such temporary abridgements as the price of survival. The instinct to preserve the republic trumps the instinct to preserve its abstract principles. It is a dangerous bargain, but it is being struck with remarkable equanimity.

Domestic politics, meanwhile, continues as though nothing had happened. The budget arguments, the bickering between left and right, the elaborate positioning for the 2002 midterm elections—all of this proceeds along its familiar, grinding track. But in foreign affairs, politics has virtually ceased. America has become easy to lead. Alarmingly so. Even those who distrusted George W. Bush—and there are many who still do, even now—trust him in this one domain, because there seems to be no alternative. He has vowed to "get the job done," that most archetypal American phrase, with its welding of moral purpose to practical completion. The words are banal, but they carry the whole weight of the national will behind them.

There is a casual fatalism in the air. At dinner the other night, friends who in any other year would have been discussing school boards and property taxes made jokes about being "blown to pieces." It was not gallows humor so much as an acknowledgement that fate has taken over. America must do what it must do: extinguish al-Qaida, root and branch. The idealism that has historically made America an erratic, sometimes fickle, presence in the post-war diplomacy of the world has now found a purpose that everyone can agree on. It is nothing less than the saving of America itself. What this means for the world is, as yet, unclear. But what the world means for America has not been clearer since Thomas Jefferson first articulated the nation's essential creed. It means safety—safety from sea to shining sea, and maybe not beyond.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_13
body:
The politics of law and order is entirely about spin. Not partly, not mostly, but entirely. Some of the impressions that spin creates happen to be correct: crime has doubled over twenty years, and the public does genuinely want heavier sentencing. But the raw material politicians handle is propaganda, not fact. In no other area of political conflict does impression so completely eclipse reality, and both major parties manipulate statistics to create impressions rather than confront genuine problems. The voter, fed a diet of competing fictions, is left to choke on the husks.

Begin with the statistical warfare. The Home Secretary, David Blunkett, declares that crime has fallen to its lowest level since the mid-1980s. The Shadow Home Secretary, Oliver Letwin, retorts that violent crime has doubled under Labour and that the streets have never been more dangerous. Each claim is relatively defensible, depending on what years you measure, what categories you include, and how you shade the figures. Mr Blunkett leans on the British Crime Survey, which shows a steady decline in burglary and vehicle theft; Mr Letwin prefers recorded crime statistics, which capture the steep rise in street robbery and the stubborn plateau of clear-up rates. The voter cannot possibly parse this. David Rose and Richard Ford, in their meticulous audit *Crime and Consequence* (Penguin), lay out the mixed picture: burglary down by 18 per cent, car theft down by 12 per cent, violent crime down by a modest 4 per cent—but street robbery up by 22 per cent, clear-up rates stagnant at 24 per cent, and convictions up by only 6 per cent. Sophistication, however true, will always be outgunned by simplicity, however false. The soundbite is the weapon of choice, and the soundbite is always a lie of omission.

Then there is the public ignorance upon which all this spin depends. The same British Crime Survey that ministers brandish also asks people what they think of sentencing. It finds that three-quarters of the public believe judges are too lenient and out of touch. Yet the same public is startlingly uninformed. Over half underestimate actual sentences by at least 30 per cent. They believe, for instance, that only half of convicted rapists go to prison; the real figure is 97 per cent. Similar grotesque misperceptions surround burglary and mugging sentences. These errors are not innocent. They are the foundation of the judiciary’s “soft touch” reputation—an image brainlessly cultivated by the tabloid press, which then seeps into ministerial critique and, in due course, into policy. The Home Secretary himself, when he talks of “rebalancing the system in favour of the victim”, is trafficking in a fiction that the public’s ignorance has made irresistible.

The policy record is a graveyard of these fictions. The previous Conservative government passed a criminal justice act every year, each one promising to be tougher than the last, until later statutes began undoing earlier ones because they had proved unworkable or counterproductive. The present government’s record is similarly mixed. Curfew orders and anti-social behaviour orders, though reasonable in their design, have failed conspicuously in practice: breach rates run at over 40 per cent, and enforcement is patchy. The latest reform package—allowing judges to take account of previous convictions when sentencing—is a rational idea. It addresses the analysis, well supported by research, that a small population of roughly 100,000 repeat offenders commits the majority of crime. It will spin well: the government can present it as ending the scandal of the “first-time offender” who has in fact been offending for years. The opposition, equally, will spin against it just as easily, denouncing it as a charter for vindictive judges and an assault on the principle of proportionality. Once again, the substance drowns in the noise.

But the spinners, for all their busyness, fail to address three unyielding challenges. The first is that the best policies cost money. The current package is priced at £600 million over three years. Is that sufficient to fund the new custodial regimes, the accelerated court hearings, and the increased police manpower that any serious crime strategy requires? The single most persuasive remedy, across all the confusing statistics, is a larger police force. Yet the Home Secretary has conspicuously failed to meet his own recruitment promises, and it is an open question whether the Treasury is genuinely committed or whether this is mere propaganda timed to an election expected in May 2005. The money, when it comes, will be spun as a bold investment; the shortfall, when it emerges, will be spun as a matter of prudent phasing. Neither spin will put a single extra constable on the beat.

The second challenge is the hole at the heart of the crime agenda. Politicians of both parties offer drugs as a blanket explanation for crime, but they refuse absolutely to address the drug laws as a focus for reform, despite mounting pressure from senior police officers, the Magistrates’ Association, and a succession of select committees. Decriminalising cannabis would eliminate a category of crime at a stroke and free up police and courts to deal with more serious offences. The evidence from other jurisdictions is accumulating, but the spinners dare not look at it. To do so would be to admit that a generation of punitive rhetoric has been aimed at a problem that prohibition itself has made worse. So the taboo holds, and the spin continues.

The third challenge is the prison. Britain now has the highest imprisonment rate in western Europe. England and Wales, according to the most recent international victimisation surveys, rank joint first among industrialised nations in the incidence of crime as experienced by victims. Yet no accountable politician will connect these two trends. To do so would be to question the assumption that more imprisonment means less crime, an assumption that the evidence does not support. The critiques are there, passionate and clinical, from the Lord Chief Justice, Lord Woolf, from the Inspector of Prisons, Anne Owers, and from the director of the prison service, Martin Narey. They speak of overcrowding, of the futility of short sentences, of the way prison manufactures recidivism. But the electoral auctioneers dare not change the terms of debate. They need the prison to be a symbol of toughness, not a symptom of failure.

So the spinners spin on. Their method is to appeal to the lowest denominator of opinion, however ignorant, and to do so by means that, more or less, always lie. The voter, confronted by two parties each claiming to be the true party of law and order, cannot distinguish one spinner from another. The beginning of wisdom in the politics of law and order would be for someone—a minister, a shadow minister, a backbencher with nothing to lose—to start the arduous business of addressing the truth. That would require admitting that the statistics are messy, that the public is misinformed, that money matters, that drugs policy is a disaster, and that the prison system is a scandal. It would be a lonely business. But until it is undertaken, the politics of law and order will remain what it has become: a contest of fictions, in which the only loser is the citizen who wants to feel safe.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/test/2/test_16
body:
It is almost impossible, even now, to believe that the peace process might fail. Having come so far, having travelled from the pitiless certainties of the past into a future that, however ambiguously, began to promise a different kind of life, Northern Ireland seems to hover on the edge of a disaster that would be as inexplicable as it is unnecessary. The end game is the hardest, not because the old conflicts over power and religion have returned, but because the argument is no longer about those things. It has become, instead, an argument about attitudes to mutual surrender. What is in dispute between the parties is whether the manner of their surrender can be made tolerable, which is the hardest question of all. It is about gestures and symbols, about the unspoken grammar of trust, about the irreducible residue of history that neither side can quite shake off. And the difficulty is that, on both sides, there is a form of what might be called real sincerity. Two kinds of real sincerity are now ranged against each other, and that is why the challenge may defeat them.

On the republican side, there is a sincere and historically remarkable belief that the war is over. The shift engineered by Gerry Adams and Martin McGuinness has been one of the most consequential transformations in modern Irish history. Sinn Fein-IRA, the organisation that once scorned politics as a bourgeois deviation, has moved itself onto a political track with a determination that is all the more credible for being so long resisted. It has done so, moreover, while insisting that its commitment to a "permanent peace" is "total". Adams himself makes the argument with a persistence that brooks no contradiction: republicanism has given up its constitutional claim to the territory of Northern Ireland; it is running the country, or a part of it, from inside the institutions of the Good Friday agreement; and it sits down to talk with the British government as a matter of routine. For a movement that resisted these things for decades, the U-turn is, by any standard, breathtaking. Adams's point is that decommissioning, though achievable, is beside the point. What matters, on this view, is the de facto end of violence. The guns are silent, the bombs have stopped, and politics has begun. To insist on a physical gesture of disarmament is, in the republican mind, to demand a humiliation that the other side does not require, a surrender that would strip the movement of the only dignity it has left.

The unionist position is no less sincerely held, and it, too, represents a journey that would have been unthinkable only a few years ago. The unionists have accepted republicans into power. They have sat down with them, taken collective decisions with them, begun to run the government of Northern Ireland with them. That they have done this while overcoming vast obstacles in the unionist psyche—the memory of murdered friends, the fear of a united Ireland by stealth, the deep and justified mistrust of men who were, until yesterday, commanders of terror—is a fact that should never be forgotten. The pressure on David Trimble from hate-filled supporters, from the London press that longs for failure, from his own party's irreconcilables, has been intense and unrelenting. Yet he has maneuvered past historical negatives with a statesmanship that, in another context, would be called heroic. He has lived with procrastination over decommissioning, and is prepared to swallow most of the Patten report on the RUC, that emblem of unionist identity, in the interests of a settlement. The unionists, in other words, have already made their concessions, and they are not trivial.

To an outsider not steeped in the minutiae of Ulster politics, the temptation is to say that the unionists should take one more stride. Even without a decommissioning gesture by the May 22 deadline, surely the process is too precious to lose. Having swallowed the camel—the acceptance of Sinn Fein in government, the transformation of the constitutional landscape, the effective end of the unionist veto—is Trimble now straining at a gnat? The argument that insisting on his resignation if decommissioning is not delivered puts blood on unionist hands has a certain superficial force. It suggests that the unionists, by their intransigence, would be the wreckers. But this is to misunderstand the nature of Trimble's position. His party has been brought to the water's edge, but it will not drink unless the other side makes a move that proves, in the only language that unionism can understand, that the war is truly over.

It is not, however, insensitive to talk about blame. In the hour of crisis, it is the republicans who are on trial. They have been treated, by the British and Irish governments and by much of the world's opinion, as the authentic voice of a community that has suffered. They have been given the benefit of every doubt, their sincerity has been proclaimed, their leadership has been lauded. Yet Realpolitik has reached its limit. The Adams leadership has argued, with some justice, that the IRA's commitment to peace is real because it has held. But in the real world, a promise is not enough. The IRA's words, however solemn, are the words of an organisation that has always reserved the right to define its own terms. What is needed, and what the unionists are right to demand, is the unbearable discomfort of a necessary gesture. A token handover of arms—overseen by General de Chastelaine's commission—would be substantively meaningless, in the sense that it would leave the IRA's capacity for violence essentially intact. But it would be politically and psychologically crucial. It would be proof, of the kind that politics always demands, that the republican movement has entered the world it chose to belong to. Without that gesture, everything else is bunker piety: a retreat into the old language, the old evasions, the old claim that the war was never a war and that the surrender, therefore, need never be made. Sinn Fein-IRA must make a decisive—though entirely incomplete—surrender, because politics, unlike war, is built on such imperfect but indispensable acts of faith.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/validation/2/validation_11
body:
All parties now treat ideology as a dirty word. The term is spat out with contempt by Tories scrabbling for a new leader and by a prime minister preparing a landmark speech on public services – and who is not relieved to hear it? But there is a price. The death of ideology means that all politics becomes more tangled. Government is harder to do and just as hard to follow. The silver thread that once linked every choice and determined every answer has been cut. When the left’s organising chant was public good, private bad, and the right said roughly the opposite, citizens could orient themselves. Now everything is messier.

While the practice of political trade has had no choice but to recognise this, public understanding has not caught up. It clings to the old simplicities. The mindset of ideology survives the passing of the ideological age. It is not only in newspaper headlines, straining for the familiar grid, that a centre-left government’s plans for public-service delivery are read as a privatising takeover. I suspect most people in the country see them that way too.

For this the government is largely to blame. In its first term, feeling its way forward, it was not straight. It tried to please all the people all the time, and so it tended to soothe the business world with one line about competition and innovation, while quietening its own constituency with words that said nothing fundamental would change. Its posture on health covered a spectrum of pledges from tough to tender. Its substitute for ideology was the mantra “what works”. But this became its philosophy well before it knew what worked in either health or education. It understood that what the Conservatives had done, above all in the NHS, did not work – yet it was far from certain how to replace it.

A great deal of confusion persists. Important distinctions are still not widely apprehended, partly because ministers’ own schemes reflect the complication of post-ideological government, and partly because producer unions have an interest in making things seem more threatening than they are. Enlisting private-sector skills and capital to run surgical centres or to turn around failing schools is not the same as privatisation. Very little of the public sector can be taken over by the private sector in any case. Instituting regimes of contestability – a device common in the public sectors of France, Germany, the Netherlands and other continental models – is not an assault on the principles of public service, but a minimal necessity for raising them to the standards of best practice that we are all entitled to expect.

Yesterday, the prime minister came closer to clarifying some of this than he has done before. It was the most important address he has delivered since the election. The headline will be his promise not to “flinch” from the opposition of the producer unions, but the real message was a more cogent invitation than any he has offered to step outside the ideological box. Some trade unionists are convinced he is thirsting for a fight – even seeking a set-piece combat such as Mrs Thatcher enjoyed with the miners. In truth, his words sounded as though they came from the last-chance saloon of the British version of social democracy, which has rather suddenly woken up to the danger that its core proposition might be seen to fail. It was a sobering, not a militant, address.

The case it made chimes with the experience of most people who use public services of every kind: an experience that tells them more money is absolutely necessary but not necessarily sufficient, and that reform is essential if delivery is to improve. This was an inaugural moment for the second term in another way as well. It put into practice the promise of plodding, responsible realism with which the prime minister said he would replace the deceptive spinning that was the leitmotif of 1997-2001. And it sent three signals.

First, the government has no single grand blueprint for the public services. There is no Great Plan, the temptation towards which it was drawn in the first term. Compared with some of its first-term rhetoric, the uncritical celebration of the private has actually receded. Different problems require different answers. If more than half of local education authorities are described by Ofsted as either fairly or very successful – and they are – the blanket abolition favoured by some reformers makes no sense. It makes just as little sense to insist that only NHS units may tender to run the new surgical centres merely because they are a supplement to NHS hospitals. A senior trade unionist told me yesterday he was sceptical about ministers’ ability to get down to the detail of reform. They were, he said, more at ease talking about – and endlessly revising – their elaborate institutional structures than with the tedious practicalities. He also regretted the adversarial, anti-union subtext that seemed to have crept into the discourse. But the prime minister avoided that. His real subtext was that, in a time of peace and high employment, no task of government is in greater public demand than making essential public services – the base material of a decent society – work properly.

Second, it is without doubt true that only a social-democratic government has a chance of doing this. Only the party with an incontestable belief in the primacy of collective provision can be trusted to reform delivery in ways that do not merely profit private business and ultimately harm the less advantaged. As a matter of fact, that belief may be a little more widespread than it once was: another victory, perhaps, for a new spirit of the times. Two of the candidates in the concurrent Conservative leadership contest make almost everything of their commitment to health and education, and admit that failure in this field contributed most to the public’s hatred of their party at the election. But the third candidate might yet win. He is the voice of the hard Tory right, which flirts with the minimal state and much of which favours an American-style recourse. With that line, he would be even less likely than his rivals to win the next election. Yet the state of Conservatism makes plain the danger that, without visible improvements brought about by progressive politicians, the public sector will become the source of yet deeper cynicism and despair.

Third, the challenge is in one sense impossible to meet. The prime minister said yesterday that it was “as great as any that has faced a postwar government”. And we must be aware that he has staked everything on outcomes that will, in many people’s eyes, never be satisfactory. The famous glass, whether in school or hospital, is always in danger of being half-empty. If the world economy, pulling Britain’s along with it, faces a prolonged downturn, not even the prudence of the chancellor will have been enough to preserve the required budget surplus.

All one can say at the moment is that the prime minister has set out, without glamour or spin, on the defining stage of his engagement with the realities the people most value. Even that is worth saying, however – and worth supporting.

author: Hugo Young
title: cross_topic_1/validation/2/validation_12
body:
It is a moment between moments, a pause in the political year when memory threatens to erase what just occurred and the next mandate has yet to impose its new certainties. Parliaments are not in session; ministers are on holiday or buried in departmental red boxes; the press is sated with other scandals. It is precisely such a moment, when the collective attention has drifted, that something essential can be lost. Before the next great set-piece announcement, before the manifesto promises of a second term are rolled out to smother recollection, there is time to conduct an audit not of what this government has done, but of what it has become. The question is not one of policy outcomes, tallying hospitals built or trains delayed, but of moral perception: how power has altered the eyesight of men and women who came to office professing an absolute commitment to racial equality, and are now, with no sense of contradiction, institutionalising ethnic discrimination in the immigration system. This is not a case of deliberate conspiracy; the corruption is worse than that. It is the corruption of righteous self-belief, a wall of certainty that has closed the gates of self-awareness, enabling ministers to do precisely what they once condemned without ever recognising the betrayal.

When this government was formed, its anti-racist credentials seemed beyond challenge. It was a point of honour, an article of faith, not merely a line in a manifesto. Within its first hundred days, the Home Secretary—a man whose own constituency had one of the highest minority ethnic populations in the country—abolished the notorious “primary purpose” rule that had made it near-impossible for migrant spouses from the Indian subcontinent to join their partners in Britain. He spoke with passion of the need to end the institutionalised suspicion that had attached, under the previous administration, to all such applicants on grounds of their origin. Shortly afterwards, the government convened the landmark Stephen Lawrence inquiry, and the Home Secretary, in receiving its report, declared that the shame of institutional racism exposed by the Metropolitan Police must be a catalyst for change across every arm of the state. The Prime Minister himself used the word “unimaginable” when asked whether his party could ever enact laws that discriminated on the basis of ethnicity. Leading figures in the Cabinet, many of whom had marched against apartheid and campaigned for race relations legislation in their youth, treated racial justice as the fixed star of their political sky.

Yet it is now possible, with the evidence assembled, to chart the quiet, almost imperceptible steps by which that star was eclipsed. The first betrayal arrived in the government’s own flagship race relations legislation. Tucked into a bill that extended the scope of anti-discrimination law in employment and public services was a clause exempting the immigration service from its provisions. Immigration officers, alone among public officials, were to be permitted to take decisions that differentiated between people on grounds of their race or ethnic origin. When challenged, the minister responsible—not the Home Secretary but a junior minister widely regarded as a safe pair of hands on equality matters—called the exemption a “technicality” and described the attack upon it as “bizarre”. A written ministerial order was then laid before Parliament authorising differential treatment of unspecified nationalities, the details to be filled in later by administrative fiat. The political press barely noticed; the anti-racist lobby was assured that the power would be used only sparingly, and only to help. You may search the record for an apology or a moment of doubt. You will find none.

The escalation was not long in coming. A second, more specific order named the groups. It listed Kurds, Roma, Albanians, Tamils, Pontic Greeks, Somalis and Afghans as categories of applicant who would henceforth face “more rigorous examination” as a class. No individual suspicion was required. The mere fact of belonging to one of these ethnicities was to be a marker, a presumption that the applicant was more likely to be an illegal immigrant or a bogus asylum-seeker. The order did not name nations; it named peoples. It was, in effect, an ethnic register, drawn up by civil servants and signed by a minister who once stood on platforms demanding an end to racial stereotyping.

The vicious absurdity of this measure is not hard to expose, yet it has passed into the administrative bloodstream with barely a murmur from the parliamentary party. Several of the listed groups have no corresponding passport or nation-state. Kurds are spread across Turkey, Iraq, Iran and Syria. Roma have no single country of origin. How, then, is an immigration officer to identify a Kurd or a Romani person except by appearance, name or some other inference drawn from what he sees and hears? The logic of racial profiling—the very thing the government’s own inquiry into Stephen Lawrence identified as a primary pollutant of public judgment—is here enshrined in statute. To compound the cruelty, the order contains a sub-clause that if information needed for an application is not available in a language the applicant understands, officials are under no obligation to provide it in one they do. Imagine yourself an elderly Somali woman, already terrified, being told that the questions on which her entire future depends will not be translated because the Home Office’s approved list of languages happens not to include Somali in a written form. This is not bureaucracy; it is state neglect of a kind that would once have drawn from these ministers the most eloquent condemnation.

There are two phases of deception to be recorded, and they strip away any remaining defence. During the passage of the race relations bill, ministers claimed that the power to discriminate was intended only to be used in favour of certain groups, never against. The classic example given—and it was repeated by more than one Home Office spokesman—was that of Kosovan refugees. The power, they said, would allow officials to fast-track genuine victims of persecution, to expedite claims that deserved sympathy. This was a lie. It was not an error of judgment; it was knowingly untrue. The second phase began after enactment, when the same junior minister, addressing a fringe meeting at the party conference, casually let slip that the new powers merely formalised a practice that had long existed inside the immigration directorate. “This is what we’ve always done,” he said, with a smile that suggested those who objected lacked all understanding of how the world really works. The flippancy was as instructive as the admission. Not only had the government erected a structure of ethnic selection; it was presenting that structure as so ordinary, so inevitable, that only the naive could be surprised by it.

Three conclusions follow, and each is grimmer than the last. First, ethnic selection is now systematised and official. The government has appointed a “race monitor” to oversee the operation of the new regime, but this figure is explicitly barred from examining individual cases. His role is to compile retrospective statistics, to report on patterns, to act as a kind of public relations figurehead whose existence allows ministers to say that accountability exists. It is the accountability of the weathervane, not the audit. Second, this is political cowardice of a deep and degrading kind. The entire edifice has been driven not by some considered re-evaluation of the national interest, but by a capitulation to the rhetoric of a hardline opposition figure who has made asylum the touchstone of his appeal. Day after day, this opponent has branded all asylum-seekers as presumptively fraudulent, and the government, rather than contesting that lie, has chosen to collaborate in it. It has adopted the language and now the practice of stigmatisation, hoping to neutralise an electoral threat by becoming indistinguishable from it. The discourse has shifted so far that the very act of seeking asylum is now routinely described in official briefings as a problem to be managed rather than a right to be upheld.

Third, and most painful, something has rotted in the ruling party. The backbenches have been silent. The great campaigns against racial injustice that furnished so many of these MPs with their political formation have been forgotten, or parked, or reclassified as relics of a past age. The party, in its hunger to hold power, has become as bad as its predecessor while losing—and this is the critical point—the intellectual capacity to recognise it. The lead minister, a self-styled anti-racist who keeps a photograph of the Sharpeville massacre in his Commons office, genuinely cannot see the contradiction. He has, I am told, expressed private bafflement that his commitment to equality should ever be questioned. It is the bafflement of the righteous, who have travelled so far inside their own certainty that they no longer perceive the landscape beyond the wall.

The deeper corruption, then, is not self-interested conspiracy. It is not the cynical pursuit of votes at any cost, though that element is plainly present. It is the belief—genuine, fervent, and utterly insulating—that because of who they are, and what they once stood for, they can do no wrong. Power has not corrupted their moral sensibility in any straightforward way; it has ossified it, encasing it in a carapace of self-regard through which no uncomfortable fact can penetrate. The law that has resulted is a disgrace. It would be a disgrace under any government, but the bitterest irony is this: perhaps only a party so entirely convinced of its own anti-racism would have had the nerve to smuggle such ethnic punishment past the line separating decency from shame. The rest of us, who remember what they said, are left to mourn not just a policy but a dissolution of the moral sense, and to wonder what other articles of faith will be deemed expendable before the audit is done.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_127
body:
In the small hours, when the street parties have broken up and the last of the beach barbecues have been doused with sand, Israel’s 60th anniversary is marked not by the fireworks of the modern celebration but by a fuzzy, monochrome flicker. On a channel no one watches any more, you can see them: the old television specials from the 1960s and 1970s, the kibbutz choirs lined up in serried ranks, the men in open-necked work shirts, the women in headscarves and peasant skirts, their faces beaming with a kind of earnest, uncomplicated joy. They sing the old patriotic Hebrew folk melodies, the songs of the Palmach and the early pioneers, and the audience—a crowd of workers and farmers, seated on rickety wooden chairs, their children on their laps—smiles back at them with the same unsullied pride. It is a tableau of socialist-realist kitsch, the sort of thing that now seems as distant as a newsreel from a Bucharest tractor factory. And yet, as Israel turns sixty and looks at itself in the mirror, these late-night reruns offer a more profound window into the nation’s soul than any number of glossy anniversary supplements. They remind us, uncomfortably, of what Israel once was—and force us to ask what it has become.

Israel, to its modern critics, is often caricatured as a colonial project, a European implant inserted into the body of the Middle East, a white-settler state of the kind that once ruled in Australia or apartheid South Africa. It is a charge that carries the sting of moral condemnation, but it also betrays a profound historical ignorance. To see those kibbutz choirs is to glimpse the human reality behind the abstraction: the truth is that the founders of Israel were not landlords seeking a second home, but refugees looking for any door that would open. The state was established in 1948, a mere three years after the liberation of Auschwitz, because the world finally—grudgingly, haltingly—acknowledged that a people who had been hunted to the edge of extinction had a basic right to a roof over their heads. The Zionist anthem, *Ein Li Eretz Acheret*—“I Have No Other Land”—is not a triumphalist boast but a tragic plea, one that carries a symmetry that should haunt both sides. For the Palestinians, too, can sing those words with equal, desperate passion. They, too, have no other land.

Yet the moral distinction is a real one. You see it in the story of Yossi Harel, the captain of the *Exodus*, that rustbucket ship which carried 4,500 hollow-eyed survivors of the death camps towards Palestine in 1947, only to be rammed by British destroyers and turned back to Germany, its human cargo sent once more behind barbed wire. The pioneers who landed on the shores of America or Australasia were, by and large, willing adventurers who already had a home; they came to seek a second one, or to extract a fortune. The Jews of the 1940s were the homeless man banging on the door, begging for a room to call his own. To elide the two is not just wrong in fact; it is a moral slander. Israel was not Rhodesia. It was, in its founding ethos at least, something closer to a desperate socialist commune.

And that is what the old television reruns capture with such unnerving clarity. Look at the faces in those audiences, the identical floor tiles beneath their feet—mass-produced in a single kibbutz factory that also, as it happens, manufactured every toilet seat in the country. Early Israel was less like an imperial outpost and more like a miniature East Germany, a place where socialism was the state religion and the collective was all. To invest in the new nation, foreign capitalists were once required to sell a 51% stake in their companies to the Histadrut, the all-powerful trade union congress that was, in effect, the government’s economic arm. The pioneers of the kibbutzim scorned private property, raised their children in communal dormitories, and believed, with a fervour that now seems almost extraterrestrial, that they were building a new kind of human being in a new kind of society.

That collectivism has vanished, and its evaporation is the most telling change wrought by six decades of statehood. Today, almost all the kibbutzim have privatized. Their members own their own homes and draw different salaries; their children are raised in private family rooms, not in the children’s houses where the egalitarian dream was literally nursed. The kibbutz has become a metaphor for the wider Israeli society, which has also privatized itself, withdrawing from the collective sphere into a tight, defensive cocoon. Israelis have a word for it: the *bu’ah*, the bubble. They construct these bubbles to seal themselves off from the anxieties that press in from every side, from the terror threat to the diplomatic isolation. The polling data captures the national schizophrenia: nearly 40% of Israelis believe their country faces a serious threat of total destruction, yet 83% are, by their own account, perfectly satisfied with their personal lives. In the bubble, the apocalypse is always just over the horizon—but the coffee is excellent, and the stock portfolio is doing nicely.

This retreat into the private has bred a dangerous complacency, especially when it comes to the question that has hung over the state since its birth: the quest for a lasting peace with its neighbours. As the 60th anniversary celebrations reach their crescendo, the diplomatic calendar has imposed its own deadline. The Bush administration, in the waning months of its tenure, has demanded progress in the Annapolis process, and the American president himself is due to visit. Israel’s ministers are keen to stress their devotion to the cause. Foreign Minister Tzipi Livni likes to boast that she has held talks with Palestinian negotiators even on what she calls “days of terror”, as if the fact of continuing to talk while the bullets fly is itself a mark of supreme virtue. Prime Minister Ehud Olmert’s hawkish critics, meanwhile, mutter that his peace overtures are nothing more than a theatrical distraction from the corruption investigation that is steadily tightening around his neck.

But the truth is that neither the rhetoric nor the posturing can mask a more fundamental reality: there is no genuine Israeli urgency for an accord. The signs are everywhere. When Jimmy Carter, that awkward, sanctimonious truth-teller, arrived in Jerusalem recently carrying tentative messages from Hamas and Damascus, not a single frontline Israeli minister would agree to meet him. The official line is that legitimizing Hamas would only weaken the Palestinian president, Mahmoud Abbas, the one partner Israel claims it can do business with. Yet Olmert has done absolutely nothing to bolster Abbas in the eyes of his own people: the illegal West Bank outposts remain stubbornly in place, the roadblocks choke Palestinian life, and not a single settler has been compensated to leave. The national mood is one of weary, cynical self-justification. Israelis point to the two great withdrawals of the recent past: from Lebanon in 2000, which brought only the Katyushas of Hezbollah raining down on the north, and from Gaza in 2005, which was answered by the crude Qassam rockets of Hamas. Why, the logic runs, should we take any further risks for peace when the outcome is always more violence?

And so Israel settles back into its bubble, telling itself that it can survive indefinitely without a political settlement. The separation barrier—that grim, grey snake of concrete that slices through the hills—has contained the suicide bombers, and the ingenious Iron Dome system will, in time, neutralize the rockets. The “demographic threat”, the spectre of Arabs one day outnumbering Jews between the river and the sea, is deemed an abstraction too remote to motivate painful concessions now. The economy is booming, swelling at a rate of 5.1%, and the Tel Aviv nightclubs are full. The beach barbecues will be lit, the flags will flutter from the car windows, and the fighter jets will trace their contrails in a display of martial pride.

But in the silent hours after the party is over, the old television shows continue their ghostly broadcast. Only a few insomniacs are watching now, staring at those faded images of a different country. The choirs are singing a melody that sounds, today, more clapped-out and passé than ever: *Shir L’shalom*, the song for peace. The lyrics, written in the blood-soaked aftermath of the 1973 war, plead with the listener not to wait too long, to bring the dead man home and let the living finally see the sun. For most Israelis, the song is a relic, an embarrassing artefact of a naive, collectivist age they have outgrown. But in the monochrome light of those late-night reruns, the song is also a rebuke. It is the voice of the Israel that might have been, and the Israel that still, against all the evidence, might one day yet be.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_22
body:
Apologies are strange creatures, far easier to give than to receive. The one who says sorry, after all, instantly stakes a claim on the moral high ground, hands spread in a gesture of openness and contrition. The recipient, by contrast, is boxed in: still nursing the wound, not yet ready to forgive, but damned as obstinate or even vengeful if they refuse the outstretched hand. This awkward pas de deux is familiar at any kitchen table, but scale it up to the bloodsoaked politics of Northern Ireland and it becomes a matter of life, death and the survival of an entire peace process.

That was the context into which the IRA dropped a statement last week that, whatever else it was, represented something new. On the eve of the 30th anniversary of Bloody Friday — the day in 1972 when 22 IRA bombs ripped through Belfast in little more than an hour, killing nine and injuring 130 — the organisation offered its “sincere apologies and condolences” to the families of “non-combatant” victims. The language was that of the therapist’s couch: words like “hurt”, “pain” and “grief” spooled across the page. Most strikingly, the statement made no attempt to blame the British government or the security forces for the civilian deaths, a rhetorical habit so ingrained in republicanism that its absence here was almost deafening.

The first reactions among unionists were, predictably, split. Tom Donnelly, whose 19-year-old sister was among the dead on Bloody Friday, described the apology as a “brave step” and said he hoped it signalled a genuine change. He was moved, and said so, in a way that suggested the possibility of a new beginning. But he was in a small minority. For most unionists, the statement was dismissed as “pure PR”, a cynical ploy dressed up as remorse. And it is not hard to see why.

For the political stakes of that apology were almost indecently high. The following Wednesday, the British government is obliged to declare whether the IRA is honouring its ceasefire. If the verdict is no, David Trimble will demand the expulsion of Sinn Féin from the Northern Ireland executive, a move that would collapse the power-sharing institutions and plunge the peace process into its gravest crisis since the Good Friday Agreement was signed. The timing of the IRA’s statement, so close to that deadline, looked less like a spontaneous act of atonement and more like a carefully aimed arrow meant to pierce the heart of the British assessment.

Unionist faith in the peace process has already drained away to almost nothing. From the beginning, many in the unionist community saw the 1998 accord not as a historic compromise but as a moral outrage: they were asked to share power with unrepentant terrorists, to see killers walk free from prison, to accept that the badge of the Royal Ulster Constabulary would be scraped off. They tolerated it only because they believed, or hoped, that republicans would eventually leave violence behind for good. That hope has been systematically dashed. The arrest of three IRA men in Colombia, allegedly training the FARC narco-guerillas; the break-in at Castlereagh police station, blamed on republicans; the ongoing street violence in Belfast — each has been another nail in the coffin of unionist trust. If assembly elections were held tomorrow, Ian Paisley’s anti-agreement Democratic Unionist Party would overwhelm Trimble’s Ulster Unionists. The apology, whatever its content, landed in a unionist community already bone-weary and suspicious.

This is why unionists are now demanding something more tangible. They want an envoy appointed by George W. Bush to assess ceasefire compliance, an independent arbiter whose verdict they hope would reassure their base and buy Trimble precious time. It is a plea born of desperation, a recognition that only an outside authority can break the deadlock of mutual mistrust.

Republicans, predictably, reject the idea with a kind of jesuitical rigour. Nothing that is not explicitly written in the Good Friday Agreement can be countenanced; the envoy plan, along with any “widening” of the ceasefire definition, is dismissed as a unionist trap. They expect Tony Blair to produce a verdict that is ambiguous enough to keep Sinn Féin inside the tent — and the choreographed dance of the last few days, with statements emerging in careful sequence from the IRA, Dublin and London, suggests a script has indeed been written.

And yet, even in such a cynical atmosphere, it would be a mistake to dismiss the apology as only a tactic. For a movement that still regards itself as an army of national liberation, whose cause it deems wholly just, to say sorry to the families of those it killed is a very big deal. It is barely a decade since Sinn Féin spokesmen could not even bring themselves to utter the word “regret” without twisting their mouths into contortions. Now the IRA itself, the organisation that planted the bombs and pulled the triggers, has apologised. That is not nothing.

Try a thought experiment. Fast forward to September 2031, the thirtieth anniversary of the 9/11 attacks. Imagine that al-Qaida’s ruling council issues a statement of regret — not for the act itself, but for the “non-combatant” victims who died in the Twin Towers. Would it heal the wounds of the victims’ families? Almost certainly not. But imagine, too, that al-Qaida had been on a stable ceasefire for six or eight years, that its political proxies were sharing power in Kabul, that an imperfect peace was holding. Under those circumstances, would not the world feel a flicker of relief at such a statement? Would it not read, not as a trick, but as a sign that something had fundamentally shifted?

So it is with this IRA apology. It should have gone further, of course: to exclude combatants — soldiers, police officers — from the embrace of remorse is to say that some deaths were still legitimate. That is a moral failure. But ask yourself: what sinister purpose were all these supposed ploys serving? To keep republicans in government. And that, under the terms of the Good Friday Agreement, is precisely where they are supposed to be. The entire peace process — the decommissioning of weapons, the apology, the halting movement towards normal politics — serves a single end: to keep the institutions afloat now that republicanism has decided it serves its strategic interest to keep them afloat. It is not neat or perfect, and the journey from paramilitary past to political future is still incomplete. But the journey is more real with each passing month. In peace processes, as in life, sometimes you must know how to say yes.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/test/3/test_25
body:
If it ain’t broke, don’t fix it. That, for as long as anyone can remember, has been the stout, commonsensical defence of Britain’s unwritten constitution. Why meddle with a patchwork of custom, precedent and royal prerogative that has served the nation for centuries? The argument has a seductive, fireside wisdom to it, conjuring images of a sturdy old oak that has weathered every storm. Tinker with it, the traditionalists warn, and you risk bringing the whole thing crashing down. Better to leave well alone, to trust in the genius of gradual evolution rather than the hubris of revolutionary design.

It is a powerful case. But it rests on a single, now-collapsing assumption: that the system still works. The test of any constitution is not its antiquity or its poetry, but the quality of governance it delivers. And on that measure, the British constitution is not merely wheezing; it is failing, demonstrably and dangerously. The moth-eaten garb of the past no longer fits. A new century, and a new millennium, demand a new, written constitutional settlement. Without one, the union itself may unravel, and Britain will be left hopelessly ill-equipped to answer the great, swirling questions of sovereignty that now dominate the world.

The evidence of breakdown is not hidden in obscure academic journals; it is etched into the collective memory of the past fifteen years. Run the reel. The pensions mis-selling scandal, which saw millions robbed of security in old age. The BSE crisis, a catastrophe born of cozy, closed-door relationships between Whitehall and the farming lobby, which ended with millions of cattle slaughtered and a nation’s faith in its food supply shattered. The relentless drip of parliamentary sleaze, from cash-for-questions to the Ecclestone affair, which painted Westminster as a bazaar where influence was for sale. The rail privatization fiasco, a botched, ideologically-driven carve-up that left a fragmented, unsafe network and, in time, a trail of fatal crashes. The poll tax, that disastrous act of political arrogance, which sent citizens onto the streets in protest and a prime minister into early retirement. And, lest we forget, the Millennium Dome, a monument to hubris that managed to squander hundreds of millions of pounds in a single, spectacular act of mismanagement.

It is tempting to see these as isolated blunders, the inevitable mistakes that any government might make over a decade and a half. But that is a cop-out. These were not random errors; they were systemic failures. Each one exposed the same, deep flaw in the British state: the absence of meaningful checks and balances, the lack of any real scrutiny, the concentration of unchecked power in the hands of a sovereign executive. The Scott inquiry into arms-for-Iraq did not simply blame a few rogue ministers; it laid bare a culture of secrecy and the ease with which Parliament could be misled. The Phillips report on BSE did not just point to scientific error; it condemned a system of governance that put the interests of producers before the safety of the public, with no mechanism for independent challenge. The problem is not this or that administration, not the Tories or New Labour. The problem is the constitution itself.

And the public knows it. The collapse of confidence in our governing institutions is no longer a matter of anecdote; it is a cold, hard fact. In 1985, according to the British Social Attitudes survey, nearly half the population expressed confidence in the House of Commons. By 1995, that figure had halved. A European Union poll in 1996 found that Britons’ faith in their own parliament ranked near the very bottom of all member states, down in the cellar with the Italians and the Greeks. Local election turnout has been plummeting for years, with some wards now struggling to attract even one in five voters. A Mori survey revealed that 71% of first-time voters believed their ballot would make little or no difference. The most damning statistic of all, perhaps, came from a Leeds by-election where participation fell below 20%. Citizens are not apathetic; they are voting with their feet. They have looked at the system and concluded, rationally, that it is not worth the candle.

For a long time, the conservative response to this rot was to insist that nothing could be done. The constitution was a mystical, untouchable thing, a seamless web that would unravel at the slightest pull. But since 1997, that myth has been shattered, and by Labour’s own hands. The constitutional reforms enacted by this government are so radical that historians must reach back to the 1830s, to the Great Reform Act and the municipal revolutions that followed, to find a precedent. Devolution to Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland has transformed the territorial shape of the state. The hereditary peers, who sat in the legislature by accident of birth, have been all but banished from the House of Lords. The patchwork quilt is already being unpicked. And once you start pulling at the threads, you cannot pretend the old garment can simply be left as it was. The conservative argument is dead. The constitution can be changed, it has been changed, and now it must be changed properly.

**Four Nations**

Devolution has forced Britain to confront a question it has ducked for three centuries: what kind of union is this? For generations, Britain acted as a unitary state, governed from Westminster, with a single, centralised sovereignty. Devolution has exposed the truth: that Britain is a multi-national entity, a nascent federation in all but name. But Labour’s ad hoc, asymmetric decentralization has opened a box of conundrums without providing any answers. What about England? Should the Westminster parliament now serve as a federal legislature for the whole kingdom, or should it double as an English parliament? Why does Scotland enjoy a measure of self-government denied to the English regions? And, most famously, why should Scottish MPs at Westminster be allowed to vote on matters affecting English schools and hospitals, when English MPs have no say over the same matters in Scotland? The West Lothian question, once a philosophical puzzle for constitutional bores, is now a live, festering wound. The worms were always in the can; now the can is wide open.

**Shattered Mystique**

Nowhere is the incoherence of the current half-reformed settlement more starkly illustrated than in the House of Lords. The removal of most hereditary peers was presented as a great democratic stride. In truth, it has done nothing of the sort; it has merely exposed the raw, undemocratic nerve at the heart of the second chamber. When the hereditaries dominated, the Lords could be dismissed as a Ruritanian joke, a Gilbert and Sullivan oddity that no one took quite seriously. Now that they are gone, the British people can see the new chamber for what it is: a body in which not a single member has been elected. The old Tory position—leave well alone—is, as I say, deceased. Once you start tinkering, you must finish the job, and finish it democratically. Even William Hague, sensing the shift in the wind, has started flirting with radical ideas like an English parliament. The current hybrid, a fudge of ancient custom and New Labour modernization, risks being the worst of all worlds: a system that has lost the mystique of tradition without gaining the legitimacy of democracy.

And all this is unfolding against a global backdrop that makes constitutional renewal not merely desirable but urgent. Next month, European leaders will gather in Nice to haggle over the future shape of the European Union. The agenda is nothing less than a fundamental recasting of the relationship between nations and the supranational institutions that govern them: majority voting versus the national veto, a common currency, a shared military force, a basic law for Europe. Meanwhile, the streets of Seattle and Prague have erupted with a new kind of protest, as a global anti-capitalist movement demands to know who rules the world—elected governments or the corporations and the World Trade Organisation. In every arena, the debate turns on a single, explosive word: sovereignty. Who should be in charge? This is the question for the world, for Europe, and, with a piercing urgency, for Britain.

These three pressures—the old system’s manifest failures, Labour’s incomplete and contradictory reforms, and the global sovereignty debate—converge into a single, irresistible case. Britain needs a new constitutional settlement, and it needs it in writing. A codified constitution would not be a panacea, but it would at least begin to address the governance deficit that has plagued us for decades. It would establish clear rules, enforceable limits, and genuine checks and balances. It would complete Labour’s unfinished business, resolving the West Lothian question, giving England a voice, and creating a second chamber that is at once legitimate and effective. It would, crucially, prevent the union from flying apart, by defining a new, federal relationship among the four nations that is coherent and just.

Most profoundly, a written constitution would equip Britain to face the outside world with clarity. We cannot hope to define our relationship with Europe, or to play a meaningful role in shaping the new global architecture, while we remain hopelessly confused about our own internal sovereignty. We cannot argue for democratic accountability abroad while we tolerate an elective dictatorship at home. The moth-eaten garb of the past, with its crown prerogatives and arcane conventions, no longer fits a modern, democratic nation. The new century demands a new settlement, one that places the citizen, not the crown, at the heart of the state. The time for patching up the old quilt is over. It is time to weave a new one.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/train/3/train_27
body:
It is the season for looking back, and for the lash of accountability. Schools hold prize-givings, hospitals face their inspectors, and every corporation in the land subjects itself to a thorough performance review. Why should a columnist be exempt? Exactly one year ago, in a fit of hubris disguised as festive sport, I published six reckless predictions for the year about to unfold. Now, with the old year crumpled like wrapping paper, is the moment to see where I hit the mark and where I missed so badly that the target might as well have been in a different postcode.

1. The judicial inquiry. Last January, all of Westminster was holding its breath for the report of Lord Justice Hartley into the government’s conduct during the Sandford affair. The conventional wisdom was that Hartley would spread the pain evenly, faulting both the government and the media for their roles in the scandal. I bought that line. I predicted that the prime minister would survive, and that the state broadcaster would be rapped on the knuckles for slack editorial practices. Both turned out to be true. But I also forecast that the judge would steer clear of the most explosive ground, that he would not dare, as an unelected figure, to deliver a verdict that could topple an elected leader. That was wrong. Hartley did not spread the pain evenly; he took a flamethrower to one side and left the other barely singed. On the central controversy — whether the government had knowingly misled the public — he ruled with a boldness that left the PM rattled but upright, and the media entity in question so comprehensively vindicated that its critics were left stammering. I had underestimated the judge’s appetite for a fight, and his willingness to pick one.

2. The US election. Here I called the winner right, but I might as well have flipped a coin. I was certain that the Democrats would nominate Governor Martin Greer, the fiery populist from the Midwest, and that he would go on to be beaten by the Republican incumbent. The first part of that prediction was so wrong it was almost elegant. Greer’s campaign collapsed in the snows of New Hampshire, and the nomination went instead to a senator I had barely mentioned in my column. I then compounded the error by insisting that the new nominee would be a sacrificial lamb. He wasn’t. The president won re-election, as I said he would, but only by a whisker, and only after a contest that was far closer than anything I had imagined. When the dust settled, I looked like a man who had correctly predicted the final score of a football match but had named the wrong goalkeeper, the wrong striker, and the wrong formation.

3. The military occupation. A year ago, there was talk of victory. The capture of the dictator had prompted a wave of giddy optimism, and the occupying power’s officials were hinting at a timetable for elections and a phased withdrawal of troops. I was having none of it. I wrote that the violence would continue, that the dictator’s capture proved nothing about the insurgency, and that the talk of exits was mere electoral calculation. All of that was right. But I then went further, predicting that the domestic political calendar would force the occupying power’s hand, that elections would indeed be held, and that troops would begin coming home in time for the campaign season. None of that happened. The occupation ground on, the elections were repeatedly postponed, and the troop numbers barely budged. I had correctly identified the cynicism, but I had not been cynical enough.

4. The intractable regional conflict. I sensed, as many did, that the old approach of the incumbent leader was being seen as futile, even by his own generals. A consensus was emerging that the status quo was unsustainable. I predicted that this pressure would lead to a change — but I was wrong about the architect. I thought the prime minister would be toppled by the corruption scandals swirling around him, and that a more hawkish successor would take his place. Instead, the man himself pulled off the most audacious move of his career, launching a new strategy that confounded his allies and enemies alike. He remains in office, and the investigations that looked so dangerous twelve months ago have fizzled into nothing. I had spotted the public mood, but I had missed the capacity of a cornered leader to reinvent himself.

5. The domestic opposition. I wrote that the opposition leader would have a solid year, and at first glance I was right. His party topped the poll in the nationwide elections, and the headlines were, for once, kind. But the numbers told a different story. The vote share was historically dismal, a fact that his internal critics seized upon within hours of the result. The party may have come first, but it was a first achieved by default, not by enthusiasm. A solid year? Only if you measure solidity by the absence of a leadership challenge, and even that is not guaranteed.

6. The domestic figures. Here, at last, I can claim two clean hits. I predicted that the maverick politician would be readmitted to his party, and that he would cruise to re-election as city mayor. Both happened. And I said that the powerful cabinet figure would remain in his post, and wait. He is still there, still waiting, and the waiting has become a kind of political art form. If only the rest of my crystal ball had been so clear.

What lessons to draw from this humbling ledger? The first is that I should have distrusted the conventional wisdom of political insiders. The entire Westminster village told me that Martin Greer was unstoppable, and I believed them. The second, and more surprising for a journalist, is that I was insufficiently cynical. I expected institutions of accountability to function — the judge to be cautious, the corruption inquiry to have teeth. They did not. The year’s great theme turned out to be unaccountability.
