Powerful figures, caught in the glare of failure or wrongdoing, simply shrugged and carried on. No senior heads rolled.

Which brings me to the two lessons the year taught me. First: don’t expect accountability mechanisms to work. Second: don’t make predictions. The world is too turbulent, too absurd, too prone to events that no one could foresee. As if to prove the point, the year ended with the cabinet minister for industry being photographed in a Brighton hotel room with a pair of handcuffs, a bowl of custard, and a man dressed as a badger. Nobody, not even the most reckless of columnists, could have predicted that.

author: Jonathan Freedland
title: cross_topic_1/train/3/train_31
body:
The guardians of our civic probity have spoken, and the message is clear: there is nothing to see here. You may move along now. The latest dispatch from the great parchment rolls of the state comes in the form of a cash-for-passports inquiry, which examined the curious case of billionaire donors to a cherished national project who found their immigration paperwork accelerated through the system at an unearthly speed. The inquiry, peering through its pince-nez, has concluded that it can find nothing “improper”. A collective sigh of relief, then, that the mechanisms of oversight are working so smoothly. It joins a growing library of exoneration. There was the parliamentary sleaze committee, that solemn body of the great and good, which investigated a senior minister and cleared him of nearly all the charges laid against him. The one minor violation it did manage to find was deemed so trifling that it declined to impose any punishment at all, a verdict that managed to be both a rap on the knuckles and a soothing pat on the head. And let us not forget the Downing Street intervention in a planning application by a billionaire arms broker, a case of such delicate timing that the prime minister’s office felt compelled to step in. This was later dismissed as a mere expression of concern over “timing”, a bureaucratic hiccup, not a favour. The organs of the state have examined the state and found it to be in ruddy good health. All is well.

And yet. If the rules, meticulously applied, say that nothing wrong happened, why does the smell of the thing linger so stubbornly in the nostrils? Why does the collective public instinct recoil from these findings, not with a satisfied nod, but with a grimace of recognition? The formal verdicts are delivered in the sterile language of compliance, but they land in the gut with the thud of a familiar, ugly truth. This is the essay’s central question: if the official definition of propriety is so at odds with the common-sense understanding of fairness, perhaps it is not the public’s nose that is faulty, but the definition itself.

Forget the finely-parsed conclusions of the inquiries. They are not the answer; they are a symptom of the disease. Strip away the legalese and the painstakingly neutral tone, and these are all versions of the same story. Someone with a colossal amount of money received a special, fast-tracked, VIP brand of treatment that is not available to you or me. Take the passport case. Even the report that grants the official absolution serves as a damning piece of evidence for the prosecution. It documents, in black and white, how the billionaire’s application was not left to the usual bureaucratic drudgery but was personally championed by the head of the immigration directorate. Residency rules, those immovable pillars for the ordinary applicant, were bent to the point of architectural impossibility to accelerate the process. A veritable roster of the great and the good, a parade of former prime ministers and senior ministers, were marshalled to lobby on the applicants’ behalf. The report calls this sequence of events “proper”. The public, possessing a more reliable moral compass, knows what it really is. The pattern reinforces itself. A Formula One boss makes a large donation to the governing party and, with the mystifying logic of a fairy tale, finds himself the beneficiary of a priceless exemption from a tobacco advertising ban. A party treasurer, his pockets bulging with massive donations, is mysteriously transmuted into a lord of the realm. The inescapable conclusion is that the officials’ definition of “proper” is the very problem that needs fixing. We need a new one.

The standard, weary remedies are wheeled out with the predictability of a ministerial resignation. The first, and most frequently prescribed, is the state funding of political parties. The theory is elegant: free our politicians from the need to grovel, cap in hand, to millionaires, and the millionaires will lose their noxious leverage. But it is a cure that fails to reach the disease. It would not have prevented the passport case, for the simple reason that the billionaires in question were not donating to a political party. Their largesse was directed at a cultural project, the Millennium Dome, a sinkhole of national vanity. The transactional favour followed the money, not the party label. State funding, moreover, carries its own risks. It has a tendency to calcify the status quo, to starve new, insurgent political movements of the resources they need to challenge the established order, creating a cartel of the comfortable. The second remedy is better regulation, trusting in the solemn power of parliamentary committees. But this is to believe in a phantom. The very committee that just investigated the minister had to drop the bulk of its charges, not because the charges were false, but because the minister simply refused to cooperate. The committee was powerless, a paper tiger whose roar was a squeak. The regulatory route is a structural dead end, a maze designed by the very people it is meant to police.

We need a more radical remedy, one drawn from the law. The core offense here is not a breach of a specific parliamentary rule, but a violation of a foundational principle: that everyone should be equal under the law. The grotesque spectacle of our age is the two-tiered reality of British life, where there is one rule for the rich and another for everyone else. To see how this might be challenged, we must look across the Atlantic, to a nation whose founding texts are more explicit on the matter. The United States’ 14th Amendment, with its majestic guarantee of “equal protection of the laws”, has underpinned the great civil rights litigation of the modern age. From Brown v. Board of Education in 1954 onward, it has been the sword and shield of citizens who petition the courts to outlaw the preferential treatment of one group over another. It is the legal mechanism that allows a principle to be enforced.

What is not widely enough understood is that Britain now has its own analog. It is Article 14 of the European Convention on Human Rights, incorporated into our domestic law through the Human Rights Act. The article bars discrimination on grounds of sex, race, colour, and “any other status”. That beautifully elastic phrase, “any other status”, is the key that could unlock a revolution. Wealth, or the lack of it, could be encompassed within it. The implications are profound. Imagine a scenario where two ordinary citizens, their applications languishing in the bureaucratic murk, petition the courts to ask why their passport was not handled with the same frantic, high-level expediency as a billionaire’s. Imagine a small, struggling developer, tangled in planning red tape, complaining that his decision was not subject to the same “concern over timing” from Downing Street as a billionaire arms broker’s. Imagine a modest business owner, denied a policy exemption that was casually granted to a wealthy donor, claiming discrimination. The argument would be simple: the only tangible difference between the treatment of these two cases is money.

A leading human rights lawyer, one who now occupies a prominent role in politics, Keir Starmer, has confirmed that discrimination against those with less money could fall squarely within Article 14’s prohibitions. The hurdle, as ever, is the law’s exacting standard of proof. The claimant would need to amass a body of evidence that is rarely lying around in plain sight, and they would have to prove that the cases were genuinely comparable in every respect bar the size of the bank balance. It is a high bar, but it is not an impossible one. And at the very minimum, such a challenge would force the authority in question to come out of the shadows and explain itself. It would have to stand in the light and justify, with a coherent argument, why it dispensed its differential treatment. The very act of forcing that justification into the public domain would be a powerful disinfectant.

The experts always told us that the Human Rights Act would make a big difference, a claim that has often seemed like a hollow promise. In the United States, the Bill of Rights has long been the engine for advancing civil rights causes that politicians, twitchy about the polls, found too politically radioactive to touch. Now it is Britain’s turn to use the same tools. For those who are sick to the back teeth of the fast tracks, the special favours, and the quiet nods to the already powerful, the politicians are too compromised to be the solution. They are the problem’s co-authors. The time has come to stop pleading with the political system to police itself. It is time to go to court.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/test/4/test_132
body:
When I first arrived in Washington as a correspondent, the late Alistair Cooke took me to lunch and, over a long and generous afternoon, gave me the best piece of advice I ever received about covering American politics. “The hardest thing for British people to grasp,” he said, leaning forward with that familiar, gravely courteous intensity, “is how important judges and courts are in this country. We think we see the world through the same eyes. We don’t. In America, the law is not just a profession; it is a branch of government, and sometimes the most important one.” I thought of that advice this week, recalled not only because John McCain was delivering the first Alistair Cooke memorial lecture in London, but because George Bush was crossing the Atlantic for a G8 summit — yet the topic that was dominating Washington, consuming the airwaves and the front pages, had nothing to do with global poverty, climate change, or Iraq. It was a resignation, announced over the Fourth of July weekend, of a 75-year-old woman from Arizona.

The departure of Justice Sandra Day O’Connor from the Supreme Court of the United States is not merely a major Washington story. It is a potentially seismic national political event, of a kind that has no parallel in British public life. In its own context, it is at least as important as electing a president or a new Congress. The nine justices of the Supreme Court are the ultimate arbiters of the American constitution, and the appointment of a single new member can reshape the law of the land for a generation. It has been eleven years since the last vacancy, the longest gap since the 1820s, and O’Connor herself was the most significant swing voter on the court for the past decade and a half. Her pragmatic, cautious centrism decided cases on abortion, race, religion, and the limits of federal power — shaping the daily lives of millions of Americans in ways that no British judge, however eminent, can ever do.

The political stakes are correspondingly immense. For the American right, this is a moment of almost apocalyptic opportunity — the chance, at last, to cement a solid conservative majority on the court, to dismantle the legacy of the New Deal and the Great Society, and to enshrine a jurisprudence of original intent under the intellectual leadership of Antonin Scalia. The Republican lobbies have been preparing for this moment for years, and the president, with no further election to face, may well share their ambition. Yet there are countervailing pressures. Most Americans are decent and pragmatic, offended by the right’s culture wars, as the opinion polls consistently show and as the Terry Schiavo case so recently reminded them. Republican candidates facing mid-term elections next year may be uneasy about the nomination of a full-throated culture warrior. And Bush himself, who has never been wholly indifferent to the political middle ground where O’Connor’s cautious pragmatism played so well, will face considerable pressure to nominate another woman — a gesture that would, at a stroke, acknowledge the symbolic importance of the vacancy while also dampening the ferocity of the confirmation battle to come.

The contrast with Britain could hardly be more pointed. Let me pose a general knowledge question. Who is Britain’s newly appointed lord chief justice, taking over from Lord Woolf on October 1? The answer — Nicholas Phillips, Lord Phillips of Worth Matravers, who has presided over the court of appeal for the past five years — will be known to vanishingly few readers. And what of the other senior appointments announced alongside his? Sir Anthony Clarke becomes the new master of the rolls, and Sir Igor Judge — surely the finest name on the English bench, a name that cries out for a novel — succeeds Lord Phillips as president of the Queen’s Bench Division. These three major appointments, between them shaping the direction of the English courts for years to come, were announced quietly in a press release that contained almost no information about the appointees beyond their names and previous offices. The announcements received only cursory media attention, appeared on no front pages, prompted no follow-up coverage, and generated no public debate whatsoever about whether these were the right people for the posts, where they stood on significant legal issues, or where their appointments might take the English courts.

This silence is striking, and eloquent. It is all the more so because these were the first senior judicial appointments to be made under the major changes brought in by the Constitutional Reform Act 2005 — the legislation that abolished the ancient office of lord chancellor as head of the judiciary, created a new supreme court to replace the appellate committee of the House of Lords, and established an independent judicial appointments commission. One might have expected, in the wake of such reforms, that these appointments would be marked in a way that signalled to the public that here were key figures in a new constitutional era, chosen through a process of unprecedented openness and accountability. In the event, nothing of the sort occurred. The most senior appointments in the supposedly reformed judicial system were made exactly as they always have been — unheralded, unexamined, and with minimal public engagement.

The American process, by contrast, is about to become a national spectacle. Some fifty million dollars is reportedly expected to be spent in the battle over O’Connor’s seat, and with two other justices in their eighties and a third a cancer sufferer, the entire drama may soon be repeated with even greater intensity, expense, and epochal consequences. From a distance, this can seem a travesty — even a corruption — of a process for selecting senior members of an independent, merit-based judiciary. The televised hearings, the attack ads, the labouring of single phrases from decades-old judgments, the reduction of a jurist’s life’s work to a single vote on a single emotive issue: all of this can appear to degrade the very idea of judicial independence.

And yet, for all its mess and its money, the American process does at least explicitly and implicitly recognise the centrality of judges and the law to the constitutional processes of modern democratic government. It acknowledges that the highest courts are not merely technical bodies resolving private disputes, but authoritative interpreters of the values that bind the nation. It proceeds from the understanding that the people, through their elected representatives, have a legitimate interest in the judicial philosophy of those who wield such power over their lives. The British system, by contrast — with rare and distinguished exceptions, such as the outgoing Lord Woolf, who used his office to speak out on matters of public concern — remains overwhelmingly in denial about these connections. It clings to a fiction of judicial anonymity, a belief that the best judges are those whose names we never learn and whose thinking we never probe.

If citizens meekly concede that they have no right to know about the highest judges in the land, or to call their thinking to public account, then they vacate an important part of the public arena. And this is not a merely academic loss. For unless we can respect judges, it is harder to respect the law; and unless we respect the law, it is harder to respect the system of government of which both law and judges are integral parts. That, in the end, is the lesson Alistair Cooke was trying to teach me, and which Britain still stubbornly refuses to learn.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/test/4/test_138
body:
There was a time, not so very long ago, when the Republican Party was a broad church, a coalition of often warring traditions that somehow held together, election after election, in the service of the national interest. It was a party that contained, within its capacious ranks, the grand, interventionist, "compassionate conservative" tradition of Theodore Roosevelt and Nelson Rockefeller, who believed in the power of government to do good; and it also contained the more modest, restrained, even sceptical tradition of Dwight Eisenhower and Richard Nixon, men who understood the limits of American power and the dangers of ideological overreach. The old Republican Party was an alliance of interests, not a crusade of faith. It was a party of business and of the suburbs, of the farm and the factory, and it drew its strength from its ability to manage the contradictions of a diverse nation. The tensions between these two wings were real, often bitter, but they were contained, just about, by a shared commitment to a certain idea of America: pragmatic, prosperous, and secure.

Today, that party is no more. The modern Republican Party is an alliance of a very different sort: an alliance between a dominant, aggressive, and increasingly theocratic religious conservative activist wing, and a moderate electorate that, by and large, still believes the party is a benign, mainstream force. The transformation has been gradual, but its apotheosis came, with a kind of terrible clarity, at the 2004 Republican National Convention in New York. That convention, held in the shadow of September 11, was a masterclass in the politics of orchestrated hostility, and it revealed, to anyone who cared to look, the true nature of the party that George W. Bush had built.

The personal attacks on John Kerry, the Democratic nominee, were not a sideshow; they were the main event. The convention floor was a sea of delegates wearing Purple Heart band-aids, a deliberate and contemptuous mockery of Kerry's Vietnam war wounds. The message was unmistakable: this man's service, his sacrifice, his very body, were a joke. And the platform speeches were, if anything, even more brutal. The most memorable—and the most instructive—was delivered by Zell Miller, the Democratic senator from Georgia who had somehow become a Republican attack dog. Kerry, Miller thundered, was "wrong, weak and wobbly," a man who could not be trusted with the defence of the nation. The words hung in the air, a distillation of the new Republican style: personal, vicious, and unencumbered by any sense of decency.

What made this spectacle so remarkable was the contrast it offered with the non-combatant status of the men at the top of the ticket. George W. Bush and Dick Cheney, the architects of the party's post-9/11 militarism, had both avoided service in Vietnam with a kind of disdainful ease. Bush, through a string of family connections, had found a safe billet in the Texas Air National Guard; Cheney, when asked about his own deferments, had icily noted that he had "other priorities" during the war. Here was the true hypocrisy of the modern Republican Party: not that it revered military service, but that it sought to claim exclusive ownership of the politics of defence and security, while reserving the right to mock and belittle those who had actually served, if they happened to be on the other side.

The convention was, in this sense, a perfect exercise in double-speak. The military was venerated in the abstract, but never, for a moment, subjected to criticism. The horrors of Abu Ghraib, which had broken just a few months earlier, were simply not mentioned. The torture chambers, the photographs, the systematic degradation of prisoners—all of it was airbrushed out of existence. The party of patriotism had decided that the best way to honour the troops was to pretend that they could do no wrong. It was a strategy that would have been familiar to any student of totalitarian politics, and it was, in its own way, deeply revealing.

One could not help but wonder, during those four days in New York, what would have happened if the political tables had been turned. If Al Gore had been president during the attacks of September 11, would the Republicans have rallied around him, as the nation had rallied around Franklin Roosevelt after Pearl Harbor? The question answers itself. The Republican Party of 2004 would have done no such thing. It would have blamed Bill Clinton, with a ferocity that would have made the impeachment saga look like a tea party. The proof was already there, in the pages of the books that were then piled high in the Barnes and Noble stores of the city. "Losing Bin Laden," "Dereliction of Duty," "Intelligence Failure"—the titles alone were a bill of indictment, and they were aimed not at the terrorists, but at the Democrats. The message was clear: in the Republican imagination, the enemy was not just abroad; it was at home, and it wore a blue tie. And if John Kerry were to win the election, the Republicans would treat him not as a legitimate president, but as a usurper, to be opposed at every turn, and to be impeached at the first opportunity. The impeachment reflex, so recently honed against Bill Clinton, would be dusted off and redeployed, with a ferocity that would make the 1990s look like a golden age of bipartisanship.

All of this, of course, was merely a prelude to the real business of the convention: the consecration of the party's fusion of military-industrial power and religious imperatives. It was a fusion that Dwight Eisenhower, the last Republican president to have actually commanded troops in battle, had warned against with a prescience that now seems almost supernatural. In his farewell address, Eisenhower had spoken of the "misplaced power" of the military-industrial complex, and of the danger that it would come to dominate American life. The 2004 convention was, in a very real sense, the political expression of that warning. The party that had once been the home of Eisenhower had become the party of permanent war, a party fuelled by testosterone-driven chants of "U-S-A, U-S-A," a party that saw the world not as a place of complexity and nuance, but as a battleground between good and evil.

And the religious dimension was never far from the surface. George W. Bush, that master of double-coding, spoke in the language of moderation and inclusion, but his words were laced with signals that the religious right understood all too well. A phrase like "culture of life" might sound innocuous to the uninitiated; to the faithful, it was a clarion call. The real message, though, was delivered not in the main hall, but at a private rally, where Senator Sam Brownback, a man of unshakeable conviction, declared that the party was engaged in a "culture war" that would determine the soul of the nation. And the convention's closing prayer, delivered by a pastor who asked for divine guidance against the "forces of evil," was a reminder that, for the modern Republican Party, the language of spiritual warfare was not a metaphor. It was a policy.

Much of that convention, it is true, was an exercise in Orwellian double-speak. The words said one thing, but meant another; the images were carefully stage-managed; the dissent was ruthlessly suppressed. But the most ominous statements, the ones that linger in the memory, meant exactly what they said. The Republican Party of 2004 was not a party of conservatism in the old sense. It was a party of radical transformation, a party that had abandoned the cautious, sceptical traditions of its past in favour of a heady brew of militarism and messianism. And if anyone doubted that, they had only to listen to the speeches, to watch the delegates, and to remember that the men who had avoided the war were leading the mockery of the man who had not.

The transformation of the modern American Republican Party, then, is not merely a story of shifting electoral coalitions or changing policy positions. It is a story of the capture of a great political institution by a faction that does not share its historic values, and that is determined to remake the country in its own image. The 2004 convention was the moment when that capture was made visible, and it was a moment that should have served as a warning. The party of Lincoln and Roosevelt and Eisenhower had become the party of Rove and Cheney and Rumsfeld. And the nation, it seemed, was barely even noticing.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_34
body:
There have been three great left-wing electoral landslides in Britain since the Victorian era. The first two, Tony Blair’s in 1997 and Clement Attlee’s in 1945, are seared into the national memory. The third, the Liberal landslide of 1906, is not. It ought to be. The man who won it, Sir Henry Campbell-Bannerman, is almost entirely forgotten today, yet he inaugurated the longest continuous period of left-of-centre government in British history — nearly seventeen years, from his own victory until the fall of the Lloyd George coalition in 1922. Neither Blair nor Attlee, nor any other leader of the progressive tradition, can match that record. And yet, as the centenary of his premiership retreats further into the past, Campbell-Bannerman remains a ghost at the feast, a name that even the historically literate struggle to place.

It is an odd oblivion. The Liberal era he launched is invariably associated with his successors, Asquith and Lloyd George, the great rivals who bestrode the Edwardian and wartime years. But the pivotal 1906 election belonged to Campbell-Bannerman alone. He was the leader who rebuilt a shattered party, turned a Tory majority of 134 into a Liberal majority of 130, and created the parliamentary conditions for the reforming governments that followed. The scale of the swing remains one of the largest in British electoral history. Yet the man himself has slipped away, as if he were merely a caretaker between the Gladstonian age and the Asquithian one. That is a mistake. He deserves to be rescued.

Part of the reason for his neglect is that Campbell-Bannerman did not fit the grim template of the modern political leader. He was not driven by demons, nor consumed by ambition. Margot Asquith, who knew everyone, called him “essentially a bon vivant, a boulevardier and a humorist”. It is hard to think of a prime minister since Disraeli of whom that could be said. His biographer John Wilson described him as easy-going, content to let well alone, and notably lacking in the kind of burning ambition that disfigures so many political careers. He kept politics in proportion. He was no workaholic. He never bore grudges. These are rare, almost subversive, qualities in a profession that increasingly rewards monomania.

But do not mistake the ease of manner for laziness or for empty pragmatism. Campbell-Bannerman was not an intellectual, but he possessed deep, unwavering Liberal convictions. They were as natural to him as his lifelong love of Balzac and Flaubert. There is a speech he gave in 1898, long before he became leader, that still reads well over a century later. Liberalism, he said, meant self-government, trust in mankind’s general sense, opposition to artificial privilege, and laws that avoid aggravating inequality and that secure every person a chance at a good and useful life. That is not a bad summary of the progressive instinct, even now. It is certainly more coherent than most of what passes for political philosophy in the television age.

There are three main reasons for admiring him, and the first is his principled stand on the Boer War. The conflict nearly tore the Liberal Party apart. Much of the party hierarchy, under Lord Rosebery, supported the war, while the radical wing opposed it with mounting fury. As the new leader, Campbell-Bannerman initially sought a middle course, trying to hold the party together. But by 1901, he had heard enough. In a speech that remains the era’s most important anti-war intervention, he condemned the Salisbury government’s “methods of barbarism” — the phrase is his, and it stung — and threw his weight behind the radicals. It was a brave thing to do, and it cost him. But it was also the right thing, and it showed that behind the bonhomie there was a spine of moral seriousness.

The second reason is his pragmatic radicalism toward the emerging Labour Party. Campbell-Bannerman liked the Labour figures of his day. He sympathised with their grievances, and he understood, perhaps better than some of his colleagues, that the future of progressive politics would have to accommodate the organised working class. Within months of taking office in 1906, he delivered the Trades Disputes Act, the law that removed the threat of legal action against trade unions and gave them the freedoms they needed to grow. Asquith and others tried to water it down. Campbell-Bannerman fought them off in cabinet and pushed through a stronger version. It became the cornerstone of British labour law for the next century, and without it the union movement as we know it might never have flourished. He did not just talk about social justice; he legislated for it.

The third reason is his political skill. Looking back from the age of the permanent campaign, it is easy to miss just how formidable a strategist he was. He led from the centre, patiently rebuilding a party that had been humiliated in the 1900 election. He exploited the Tory government’s disastrous flirtation with protectionism, the tariff reform campaign that split the Conservative Party and handed the Liberals their great issue. He did not overpromise. He did not indulge in the rhetorical excess that so often tempts opposition leaders. Most of his colleagues thought he was making a mistake. The result proved them wrong. It was a masterclass in electoral politics, and it deserves to be studied.

What of his government’s record in office? It outperformed almost all expectations. If the Tory-dominated House of Lords had not been so determined to block Liberal legislation, Campbell-Bannerman would have achieved education reform, the abolition of the multiple franchise, and trade union law reform all within his first year. As it was, the Lords’ obstruction set the stage for the constitutional crisis that followed, and the great Parliament Act of 1911 was the eventual consequence. But even in the face of such opposition, Campbell-Bannerman’s ministers proved to be genuine reformers. Haldane reshaped the army. Lloyd George tackled merchant shipping. The seeds of the welfare state were sown. It was not a government of pygmies, and it was not led by one.

Enoch Powell once wrote that all political careers end in failure, and he was writing about Joseph Chamberlain. The remark is often quoted, but it does not apply to Campbell-Bannerman, except in the most literal sense. He died in Downing Street in early 1908, having resigned the premiership on his deathbed in favour of Asquith. He went out at the top, his reputation intact, his party in power, his legacy secure. That is not failure. It is the kind of ending that most prime ministers would envy.

And yet oblivion has swallowed him. There is a rival for the title of Britain’s most obscure prime minister, Andrew Bonar Law, who was rescued from the shadows by Robert Blake’s biography “The Unknown Prime Minister”. One day, perhaps, Alec Douglas-Home will also recede so far into the past that he becomes a mere footnote. But for now, the title of the nation’s unknown prime minister belongs, unjustly, to Henry Campbell-Bannerman. He was a good man, a shrewd leader, and a true liberal. He deserves to be remembered.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_37
body:
On the morning of the 1983 general election, I found myself in a small, overheated committee room in Hackney, helping a Labour ward secretary to stuff the last of his leaflets through the letterboxes of the borough. His name was Stan, or perhaps Sid, and he was a decent, weary man who had been doing this for every election since Attlee. Over a cup of tea that tasted of the tin it was made in, he asked me what I thought would happen. I told him the Tories would win by more than a hundred seats. He looked at me with something approaching pity, as if I had been duped by the establishment media, and said that in his experience Labour would win by twenty. He was wrong, and I was right. Margaret Thatcher was returned with a majority of 144, and Stan’s world—the world of municipal socialism, of full employment, of the Attlee consensus—was, for all practical purposes, swept away for a generation.

Fourteen years later, on the morning of the 1997 election, I was standing in a hotel corridor in Sedgefield when the Labour leader himself emerged from his suite, looking, as he always did, preternaturally calm. I asked him what he thought the result would be. He paused, tilted his head, and said that perhaps a win by fifty seats was on the cards. I said I thought it would be more like 150. He gave me a smile that was both sceptical and, I think, slightly unnerved, and went on his way. In the event, of course, Labour’s majority was 179, and the 150-seat prediction—which had seemed almost hubristic at the time—turned out to be a modest underestimate. The secret, in both cases, was not some arcane psephological insight or a line to the party whips. It was simply that I believed what the opinion polls were saying. They were not infallible—they were merely pointing in a direction that most of the political class, with its instinct for the hopeful and the anecdotal, had chosen to ignore.

All of which, in a roundabout way, brings me to the present moment, and to the question that I am asked with increasing frequency, and with varying degrees of politeness, by friends, colleagues, and the occasional enraged American email correspondent. “Who,” they demand, “is going to win the US presidential election?” And I tell them, as I have told them for weeks, that John Kerry is winning. Not because I want him to win, though I do, and not because I am a paid-up member of the liberal conspiracy, which I am not, but because that is what the polls currently show. In the battleground states—Ohio, Florida, Pennsylvania—the margins are often within the statistical error, but the direction of travel is clear, and it is towards the Democratic challenger. The hostile right-wing correspondents, who call me a “limey socialist” and worse, can take whatever comfort they wish from their own echo chambers; the facts, as my old history tutor used to say, are chiels that winna ding.

But—and this is a crucial but—saying that Kerry is ahead in the polls is not the same as predicting that he will win on November 2. Polls are snapshots, not prophecies. They have margins of error, usually of about three percentage points, and they capture a moment in time that can be washed away by events. The election is still months away, and in American politics, where a single hurricane or a stray videotape can alter the national mood, months are a geological era. All I can say, and all the polls can say, is that it is close, and that Kerry is narrowly ahead. That is not a forecast; it is a description. And it is the sort of distinction that is too often lost in the cacophony of punditry, where every poll is treated as a verdict and every shift a calamity.

Here at home, the poll-believers have some explaining to do. Two recent surveys have been held up, by otherwise sensible people, as evidence that the whole enterprise is a nonsense. The Guardian’s ICM poll, published a few weeks ago, found that when voters were asked to pick from a prompted list of issues that would be most important in deciding how they vote, Iraq came bottom, chosen by just 12 per cent of respondents. The NHS, by contrast, was chosen by 59 per cent. Yet a few days later, an FT-Mori poll asked an unprompted question about the most important issue facing Britain today, and found that “defence, foreign affairs, and international terrorism” came top, at 24 per cent—rising to 38 per cent when combined with “other important issues”. The respected commentator Peter Preston confessed himself baffled by the apparent contradiction, and many others have since followed suit. It is, they say, proof that you can make polls say anything, and that the whole business is a charade.

But this is a lazy conceit, and it matters for the civic good that we stamp it out. The confusion is more apparent than real, and the explanation is not that the polls are unreliable, but that different questions produce different answers. The ICM poll asked a specific, prompted question about voting intention: which of these issues will be most important in deciding how you cast your ballot? Mori, on the other hand, asked an open-ended, unprompted question about the issue that most concerns the nation. The two are not contradictory; they are measuring different things. It is perfectly possible—indeed, it is perfectly rational—to believe that foreign affairs and terrorism are the most important issues facing the country, while simultaneously believing that they are not the issues that will most determine your vote. The NHS, education, and the economy are the things that touch our daily lives; Iraq and al-Qaida are things that happen to other people, or that we read about in the papers. The distinction is not a failure of polling; it is a reflection of the complexity of human judgment.

There is a historical parallel here, and it is one that the Labour party, in its current mood of fretful optimism, would do well to remember. In the 1980s, the polls regularly showed that unemployment was the most important issue facing the country, and that Labour had the best policies to deal with it. Yet the Conservatives won election after election, because when it came to the voting-day issues—taxes, home ownership, and above all economic competence—the Tories had an unassailable advantage. The electorate was not being irrational or inconsistent; it was simply making a distinction between the national condition and the personal choice. The same dynamic is at work today. Defence and foreign affairs may dominate the headlines and the dinner-party conversation, but when the polling booth curtain is drawn, people vote on the things that affect their own families: health, education, and the quiet, persistent anxieties of daily life. That is why Labour, for all its woes, remains ahead in the polls that ask about voting intention. And that, in the end, is why I believe they will win the next election—just as the polls say they will.

author: Martin Kettle
title: cross_topic_1/train/4/train_39
body:
It is one of the most difficult things in politics, as in life, to speak the truth against a cause you love. The true believer who turns critic risks not merely the loss of friends and influence but a kind of inner fracture, a severing of the very loyalties that gave his convictions their shape and heat. Conscience and loyalty are perpetually at war in the human heart, and that war never produces an easy winner. The instinct to stand by your side, to defend the indefensible in the name of a larger good, to see the speck in your opponent’s eye while ignoring the beam in your ally’s—these are not merely weaknesses of the weak; they are the besetting temptations of the strong, the principled, and the committed. And no figure in modern British politics illustrates the perils of undifferentiated loyalty more poignantly than Tony Blair in his long, fraught and increasingly tormented relationship with the United States of America.

Blair’s belief in the centrality of the American alliance was never a mere diplomatic convenience. It was, and remains, a conviction rooted deep in his political soul, a first principle from which so much else flows. Yet what began as a strategic insight—that Britain’s interests are best served by binding itself as closely as possible to the world’s sole superpower—has hardened over time into something more troubling: a loyalty that increasingly demands the suppression of honest judgment. Nowhere is this more painfully visible than in Blair’s continuing defence of the Bush administration over Iraq, a stance that pits his enduring instincts against a threatening and recalcitrant reality.

To understand how Blair arrived at this pass, one must go back to the 1990s and to a view of politics that he absorbed early and never abandoned. For Blair, British domestic politics have always operated downstream from America. He watched Bill Clinton’s 1992 victory with the keen eye of a man who understood that a Democrat in the White House made a Labour government in London seem not merely possible but almost providential, aligned with the grain of history. Clinton’s re-election in 1996 only deepened that conviction. By the time of Labour’s own landslide the following year, the transatlantic political weather seemed set fair. Then came the election of George W. Bush in 2000, and the weather changed abruptly. A Republican administration, mistrusted by much of the British left and instinctively unilateralist in temper, posed a serious challenge to Blair’s whole strategic architecture. His response was characteristic: he rushed to be the first foreign leader to visit Camp David in early 2001, determined to prevent William Hague—and later Iain Duncan Smith—from claiming to be the party truly in touch with the new America. Loyalty, in that moment, was already beginning to override other considerations.

But Blair’s belief in the American tie soon widened from the merely tactical to the grandly doctrinal. The United States, he came to argue, is the indispensable nation for settling global problems. Without American engagement, the world drifts towards chaos; with it, there is at least a chance of order. And the Anglo-American relationship, in this vision, is the essential catalyst, the special something that keeps America outward-looking rather than isolationist. How much of this Blair truly believes is a matter of legitimate debate. He is, after all, a politician who has always been more comfortable in European capitals than his rhetoric sometimes suggests, and his self-identification as a European is not feigned. Yet where his heart lay was made vividly clear at a White House dinner with Clinton in 1998, when he rose to quote the words that Harry Hopkins, Franklin Roosevelt’s emissary, had addressed to Churchill at a dark hour of the Second World War: “Whither thou goest I will go… Thy people shall be my people, and thy God my God. Even to the end.” It was a declaration of faith, and it came from somewhere deep.

The trouble is that the Clinton era, for all its frustrations—and Blair sometimes struggled mightily to secure American engagement even then, notably over Kosovo—was a fundamentally different proposition from the Bush era. Under Clinton, the United States operated, however imperfectly, within a broadly internationalist framework. The Bush administration, by contrast, has broken with those premises in ways that are historically exceptional. As Frances Fitzgerald observed in the New York Review of Books, no postwar administration save perhaps Reagan’s first has so thoroughly repudiated the multilateral assumptions of its predecessors. Bush himself has rarely defined his foreign-policy goals in concrete terms, preferring to speak in vague but portentous language of threats and of security solutions defined by America alone. Recall his remark during the 2000 campaign: “We’re not so sure who the they are, but we know they’re there.” It was a phrase that captured an entire cast of mind.

That cast of mind has been given hard institutional form by the men around the president. Dick Cheney, Donald Rumsfeld, and above all John Bolton have set an agenda that repudiates collective action and treats alliances as instruments of convenience rather than commitments of principle. Bolton’s notorious claim that “there is no such thing as the UN” and that the international community is merely what the United States can lead when it suits its interests was not an aberration; it was a statement of the governing philosophy. The Pentagon, meanwhile, has shifted from what it calls a “threat-based strategy” to a “capabilities-based approach,” a bureaucratic sleight of hand that allows it to demand military spending without having to justify it against any identifiable enemy. Rumsfeld’s language of defending “against the unknown” is the perfect expression of a worldview in which the only reality that counts is American power and the will to use it.

All of this constantly undermines Blair’s efforts to portray the Bush administration as a willing partner in the moral internationalist order he championed so eloquently in his Brighton conference speech and reiterated to the TUC. One can feel genuine sympathy for him. He believes in the right things—in multilateralism, in the rule of law, in the necessity of using power for humane ends—and he is genuinely trying to exert a moderating influence in Washington. But he earns grief at home and, in the end, achieves little lasting in Washington because of a profound disjunction between what he wants to believe about this administration and what is actually true. He has, in the classic manner of the over-loyalist, allowed his loyalty to override clarity and truth. The administration he defends has discarded the very rules he wants to play by, and rather than confront that reality openly, he pretends it does not exist. That is the tragedy of Tony Blair’s loyalty, and it is a tragedy that is still unfolding.
